questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Established
garrison towns to station the soldiers
B. Alauddin Khilji 2. Made the Iqta
system hereditary
C. Firoz Shah Tughlaq
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Constitution** of a country is a set of written rules that are accepted by all people living together in a country.  It is the supreme law that determines the relationship among people living in a territory (called citizens) and also the relationship between the people and government.
● Following are the functions of the Constitution: ○ It generates a **degree of trust and coordination** that is necessary for different kinds of people to live together by providing a common framework and set of rules, helping people from diverse backgrounds live together peacefully and work towards shared goals. **So, point I is correct.** ○ It specifies **how the government will be constituted**, who will have power to make which decisions, such as the **roles of the legislature** (which makes laws), the executive (which implements laws) and the judiciary (which interprets laws). This **ensures clarity,** **accountability and balance among the different branches of government.** **So, point II** **is correct.** ○ It  **limits government authority** to prevent misuse of power and defines citizens' rights, ensuring that everyone can lead a life of dignity. **So, point III is correct.** ○ It reflects the vision, values, and goals of society, aiming to create a just and inclusive community. For example, the Preamble expresses the **aspirations of Indian society** for justice, liberty, equality and fraternity for all citizens. **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
The principle of 'rule of law' as reflected in the
Constitution of India implies that:

options.a:
The State can enact laws granting immunity to
certain public officials from judicial scrutiny
in matters of governance
options.b:
All individuals, including those in authority,
are equally subject to the laws of the land and
are accountable before ordinary courts
options.c:
The Legislature may override Fundamental
Rights through ordinary legislation if public
interest so demands
options.d:
The Executive can suspend legal procedures
and court oversight during emergencies in the
national interest

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Right to Equality**:
● The Constitution of India says that the government shall not deny to any person in India **equality before** **the law or the equal protection of the laws.** It means that the **laws apply in the same manner** **to all, regardless of a person’s status.** This is called the **rule of law.** Rule of law is the foundation
of any democracy. It means that **no person is above the law.** There cannot be any distinction between a political leader, government official and an ordinary citizen.
● Every citizen, from the Prime Minister to a small farmer in a remote village, is subjected to the same laws. No person can legally claim any special treatment or privilege just because he or she happens to be an important person.
● This basic position is further clarified in the Constitution by spelling out some implications of the **Right** **to Equality.** The government shall not discriminate against any citizen on grounds **only of religion,** **race, caste, sex or place of birth.** Every citizen shall have access to public places like shops, restaurants, hotels and cinema halls. Similarly, there shall be no restriction with regard to the use of wells, tanks, bathing ghats, roads, playgrounds and places of public resorts maintained by the government or dedicated to the use of the general public. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Established
Bandagans as a political class
D. Muhammad bin Tughlaq
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Constitution of India was made by the **Constituent Assembly,** which had been elected for undivided India. It held its first sitting on **9 December 1946** and reassembled as the Constituent Assembly for divided India on 14 August 1947. Its members were chosen by **indirect election by the** **members of the Provincial Legislative Assemblies** that had been established under the **Government of India Act, 1935.** The Constituent Assembly was composed roughly along the lines suggested by the plan proposed by the committee of the British cabinet, known as the **Cabinet Mission.** **So, statement I is correct.**
● According to this plan: o **Each Province** and each Princely State or group of States were **allotted seats proportional**
**to their respective population** roughly in the ratio of 1:10,00,000. As a result the Provinces (that were under direct British rule) were to elect 292 members while the Princely States were allotted a minimum of 93 seats. **So, statement II is correct.** o **Seats allocated to each British province** were to be divided among the three principal
communities–Muslims, Sikhs and General (all except Muslims and Sikhs), in proportion to their population. o The **representatives of each community** were to be elected by members of that community
in the **Provincial Legislative Assembly** and voting was to be by the **method of** **proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote. So, statement** **III is correct.** o The representatives of the Princely States were to be nominated by the heads of the Princely
States. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Planned a military
campaign to capture Mongol territory
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● During the freedom struggle, Indian leaders strongly advocated for the protection of individual rights. As early as 1928, the Motilal Nehru Committee demanded a **bill of rights**. Naturally, when the Constitution was framed after independence, there was unanimous agreement on including and safeguarding these as **Fundamental Rights.**
● The word fundamental suggests that these rights are so important that the Constitution has separately listed them and made special provisions for their protection. The Fundamental Rights are so important that the Constitution itself ensures that they are not violated by the government.
● Fundamental Rights are different from other rights available to us. While **ordinary legal rights** are protected and **enforced by ordinary law**, Fundamental Rights are protected and guaranteed by the **Constitution of the country. So, statement I is correct.**
● Ordinary legal rights may be changed by the **legislature by ordinary process of law making,** but a Fundamental Right may only be changed by **amending the Constitution itself.** Besides this, no organ of the government can act in a manner that violates them. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Judiciary has the powers and responsibility to protect the fundamental rights from violations by actions of the government. Executive as well as legislative actions can be declared illegal by the judiciary if these violate the Fundamental Rights or restrict them in an unreasonable manner. However, **Fundamental** **Rights are not absolute or unlimited rights.** Government can put **reasonable restrictions** on the exercise of our Fundamental Rights. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 The maximum strength of Legislative
Councils in States is fixed at one-third of the total strength of the assembly and the minimum strength is fixed at 40.
II. 	 The resolution for the abolition or creation
of the Legislative Council in a State must be passed by the State Assembly of the respective State by a Special Majority.
III. 	An Act of Parliament for the abolition or
creation of Legislative Councils in States is deemed as an Amendment to the Constitution for the purpose of Article 368.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Like the Rajya Sabha, the Legislative Council is a continuing chamber, that is, it is a permanent body and is not subject to dissolution. But, one-third of its members retire on the expiration of every second year. So, a member continues as such for six years.
● Unlike the members of the Legislative Assembly, the **members of the Legislative Council are** **indirectly elected**. The maximum strength of the council is fixed at **one-third of the total strength** **of the assembly** and the minimum strength is fixed at 40. It means that the size of the council depends on the size of the assembly of the concerned State. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Constitution of India under **Article 169** provides for the **abolition or creation of Legislative** **Councils in States.** Accordingly, the Parliament can abolish a Legislative Council (where it already exists) or create it (where it does not exist), if the Legislative Assembly of the concerned State passes a resolution to that effect. Such a specific resolution must be passed by the **State Assembly by a Special** **Majority,** that is, a majority of the total membership of the assembly and a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members of the assembly present and voting. This Act of Parliament is **not to be** **deemed** as an Amendment of the Constitution for the purposes of **Article 368** and is passed like an ordinary piece of legislation (ie, by **Simple Majority). So, statement II is correct and statement** **III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The Constitution of India provides the right to convert another person to one’s own religion by transmission and dissemination of one’s religious beliefs to others.
**Statement II:** Article 25 of the Constitution of India says that all persons are equally entitled to freedom of conscience and the right to freely profess, practice and propagate religion.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct,
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct, but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect, but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● According to the Constitution of India, **everyone enjoys the right to follow the religion of his or** **her choice.** In India, everyone is free to choose a religion and practice that religion. Freedom of religion also includes the freedom of conscience. This means that a person may choose any religion or may choose not to follow any religion.
● **Article 25** says that all persons are equally entitled to **freedom of conscience and the right to** **freely profess, practice and propagate religion.** **So, statement II is correct.**
● The implications of these are:
○ **Freedom of conscience:** Inner freedom of an individual to mould his relation with God or Creatures in whatever way he desires. ○ **Right to profess:** Declaration of one’s religious beliefs and faith openly and freely. ○ **Right to practice:** Performance of religious worship, rituals, ceremonies and exhibition of beliefs and ideas. ○ **Right to propagate:** **Transmission and dissemination of one’s religious beliefs to** **others** or exposition of the tenets of one’s religion. But, **it does not include the right to** **convert another person to one’s own religion**. Forcible conversions impinge on the ‘freedom of conscience’ guaranteed to all persons alike. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **freedom of religion is not absolute** in India. It is subject to **reasonable restrictions** by the State in the interest of **public order, morality and health**. For example, the government has restricted or banned certain religious practices like **sati, bigamy, or human sacrifice** on these grounds. **So, statement–I is incorrect, but Statement–II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Written Constitution
II. 	 Independent judiciary
III. 	Single citizenship
IV. 	 Appointment of Governor
How many of the above are federal features of the
Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Governments can be classified into unitary and federal on the basis of the nature of relations between the national government and the regional governments.
○ **A unitary government** is one in which all the powers are vested in the national government and the regional governments, if they exist at all, derive their authority from the national government. ○ **A federal government**, on the other hand, is one in which powers are divided between the national government and the regional governments by the Constitution itself and both operate in their respective jurisdictions independently.
● **Federal Features of the Constitution of India:** o Dual Government (that is, national government and regional government) o **Written Constitution. So, point I is correct.** o Division of powers between the Centre and the states in terms of the Union List, the State List
and the Concurrent List in the Seventh Schedule. o Supremacy of the Constitution o Rigid Constitution o **Independent judiciary. So, point II is correct.** o Bicameralism
● **Unitary Features of the Indian Constitution** o Strong Centre o States Not Indestructible o Single Constitution o Flexibility of the Constitution o No Equality of State Representation o Emergency Provisions o **Single Citizenship. So, point III is not correct.** o Integrated Judiciary o All-India Services o Integrated Audit Machinery o Parliament’s Authority Over the State List
o **Appointment of Governor. So, point IV is not correct.** o Integrated Election Machinery o Veto Over State Bills **So, only two of the above are federal features of the Constitution of India.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
The Vice President acts as the President when
there is a vacancy in the office by reason of:
I. - death of the President
II. 	 resignation of the President
III. 	 impeachment of the President
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Vice-President** is elected by the members of an electoral college consisting of the members of **both Houses of Parliament.** The electoral college consists of:
○ It consists of both elected and nominated members of the Parliament (in the case of the President, only elected members). ○ It does not include the members of the State Legislative Assemblies (in the case of the President, the elected members of the State Legislative Assemblies are included).
● The Vice-President holds office for a **term of five years** from the date on which he enters upon his office. However, s/he can resign from her/his office at any time by addressing a resignation letter to the President. S/he can also be removed from the office before completion of his term. A formal impeachment is not required for his removal.
● The functions of the Vice-President are two-fold: ○ S/he acts as the **ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha.** In this capacity, his powers and functions are similar to those of the Speaker of the Lok Sabha. In this respect, s/he resembles the American Vice President who also acts as the Chairman of the Senate–the Upper House of the American legislature. ○ S/he acts as President when a vacancy occurs in the office of the President due to **her/his** **resignation, impeachment, death or otherwise.** S/he can act as President only for a **maximum period of six months,** within which a new President has to be elected. **So, points** **I, II and III are correct.** ○ **B. D. Jatti** acted as President on the death of **Fakhruddin Ali Ahmed** until a new President was elected. Further, when the sitting President is unable to discharge his functions due to absence, illness or any other cause, the Vice-President discharges his functions until the President resumes his office. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
In India, which one of the following best
characterises the Permanent Executive?

options.a:
Elected representatives responsible for
enacting legislation
options.b:
Judicial authorities entrusted with interpreting
constitutional provisions
options.c:
Bureaucrats responsible for formulating and
implementing government policies
options.d:
Experts and consultants appointed to assist in
policy research and project implementation

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Executive organ** of the government includes the **Prime Minister, the Ministers** and a large organisation called the **bureaucracy or the administrative machinery.** Administrative machinery is described as civil service. Trained and skilled officers who work as permanent employees of the government are **assigned the task of assisting the ministers in formulating policies and** **implementing these policies.**
● In a democracy, the elected representatives and the Ministers are in charge of government and the administration is under their control and supervision. In the parliamentary system, the legislature also exercises control over the administration. The administrative officers cannot act in violation of the policies adopted by the legislature. It is the responsibility of the Ministers to retain political control over the administration. India has established a professional administrative machinery.
● The Indian bureaucracy consists of the **All-India services, State services, employees of the local** **governments** and **technical and managerial staff** running public sector undertakings. Makers of our Constitution were aware of the importance of a non-partisan and professional bureaucracy. They also wanted the members of the civil services or bureaucracy to be impartially selected based on merit. So, the **Union Public Service Commission** has been entrusted with the task of conducting the process of recruitment of civil servants for the government of India. Similar public service commissions are also provided for the States. **Members of the Public Service Commission are appointed for a fixed** **term.** Their removal or suspension is subject to a thorough enquiry made by a judge of the Supreme Court. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
A Vote of No-Confidence against even a an
individual Minister results in the resignation of
the entire Council of Ministers.
**Statement II:**
The Council of Ministers is collectively
responsible to the Parliament Lok Sabha.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct,
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct, but Statement II is not
correct
options.d:
Statement I is not correct, but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● As the Constitution of India provides for a Parliamentary System of Government modelled on the British pattern, the Council of Ministers, headed by the Prime Minister, is the real executive authority in our politico-administrative system.
● Article 74 provides for a Council of Ministers with the Prime Minister at the head to aid and advise the President in the exercise of his functions.
● The **Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha.** This provision means that a Ministry which **loses the confidence of the Lok Sabha is obliged to resign.** The principle indicates that the ministry is an executive committee of the Parliament and it collectively governs on behalf of the Parliament. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Article 75 of the Constitution of India** clearly states that the **Council of Ministers is collectively** **responsible to the Lok Sabha**. This means that all the Ministers own joint responsibility to the Lok Sabha for all their acts of omission and commission. They work as a team and swim or sink together. When the Lok Sabha passes a no-confidence motion against the Council of Ministers, all the Ministers have to resign, including those ministers who are from the Rajya Sabha. In view of the constitutional provision regarding the collective responsibility of the Council of Ministers to the Lok Sabha, **a motion** **of no confidence can be moved only against the Council of Ministers and not against an** **individual Minister. So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **Council of Ministers cannot exist without the Prime Minister**. The Council comes into existence only after the Prime Minister has taken the oath of office. The death or resignation of the Prime Minister automatically brings about the dissolution of the Council of Ministers, but the demise, dismissal or resignation of a minister only creates a ministerial vacancy. The Prime Minister acts as a link between the Council of Ministers on the one hand and the President, as well as the Parliament, on the other. **So, statement I is not correct, but Statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Which of the following provisions are part of
the ‘Directive Principles of State Policy’ in the
Constitution of India?
I. 	 Uniform Civil Code
II. 	 Development of scientific temper, humanism
and the spirit of inquiry and reform among
citizens
III. 	Promotion of cottage industries on
cooperation basis
IV. 	 Provisions for just and humane conditions of
work
V. 	 Separation of judiciary from the executive
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:

options.c: I, III, IV and V only
options.d: I, II, III and V only

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The phrase ‘**Directive Principles of State Policy**’ denotes the ideals that the State should keep in mind while formulating policies and enacting laws. Dr. B.R. Ambedkar described these principles as **‘novel features’** of the Indian Constitution.
○ These are the constitutional instructions or recommendations to the State in legislative, executive and administrative matters.
● The Directive Principles of State Policy are enumerated in **Part IV** of the Constitution from **Articles** **36 to 51.** The framers of the Constitution borrowed this idea from the **Irish Constitution** of 1937,
which had copied it from the Spanish Constitution.
● Some of the important Directive Principles of State Policy are given below: ○ To secure for all citizens a **Uniform Civil Code** throughout the country **(Article 44). So,** **point I is correct.** ○ To promote **cottage industries** on an individual or cooperation basis in rural areas **(Article** **43). So, point III is correct.** ○ To make provision for **just and humane conditions of work** and maternity relief **(Article** **42). So, point IV is correct.** ○ To separate the **judiciary from the executive** in the public services of the State **(Article 50).** **So, point V is correct.**
● According to **Article 51A(h) (Fundamental Duties)**, **it shall be the duty of every citizen of** **India to** develop scientific temper, **humanism and the spirit of inquiry and reform.** Thus, **this** **provision is a part of the Fundamental Duties and not the Directive Principles of State** **Policy. So, point II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Andhra Pradesh
II. 	 Madhya Pradesh
III. 	Maharashtra
IV. 	 Gujarat
V. 	 Uttar Pradesh
How many of the above States in India have Bicameral Legislatures?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● There is no uniformity in the organisation of State Legislatures. Most of the States have a unicameral system, while others have a bicameral system. In the States having a bicameral system, the **State** **Legislature** consists of the **Governor,** the **Legislative Council** and the **Legislative Assembly.** The Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad) is the upper house (second chamber or house of elders), while the Legislative Assembly (Vidhan Sabha) is the lower house (first chamber or popular house).
● At present (2024), **only six States** have two Houses (bicameral). These are **Andhra Pradesh,** **Telangana, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Maharashtra and Karnataka. So, points I, III and V are** **correct.**
● The States of **Madhya Pradesh and Gujarat** have a **unicameral system. So, points II and IV are** **not correct.**
● The Jammu and Kashmir Legislative Council was abolished by the Jammu and Kashmir Reorganisation Act, 2019. The Tamil Nadu Legislative Council Act, 2010 has not come into force. The Legislative Council in Andhra Pradesh was revived by the Andhra Pradesh Legislative Council Act, 2005. The 7th Amendment Act of 1956 provided for a Legislative Council in Madhya Pradesh. However, a notification to this effect has to be made by the President. So far, no such notification has been made. Hence, Madhya Pradesh continues to have one House only. **So, only three of the above States in India have Bicameral Legislatures.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding the
advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of India:
I. 	 The Prime Minister of India may refer to
the Supreme Court any matter of public importance or involving the interpretation of the Constitution for its opinion.
II. 	 The Supreme Court, under its advisory
jurisdiction, is not bound to give its advice.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In addition to original and appellate jurisdiction, the Supreme Court of India also possesses **advisory** **jurisdiction.**
● **Article 143** of the **Constitution of India** confers advisory jurisdiction on the **Supreme Court and** **provides for the power of President to consult the Supreme Court;** it says that if it appears to the President that a question of law or fact has arisen, or can arise in future which is of public importance
and it is beneficial to obtain the opinion of the Supreme Court, s/he may refer the question for consideration and the Court may, after such hearing report to the President its opinion. **So, statement** **I is not correct.**
● The Constitution of India under Article 143 authorises the President to seek the opinion of the Supreme Court in the **two categories of matters:**
o On any **question of law or fact of public importance** which has arisen or which is likely to
arise. o On any dispute arising out of any **pre-constitution treaty, agreement, covenant,**
**engagement, sanad** or other similar instruments.
● In the first case, the Supreme Court may tender or refuse to tender its opinion to the President. But, in the second case, the **Supreme Court ‘must’ tender its opinion to the President**. In both cases, the opinion expressed by the Supreme Court is only advisory and not a judicial pronouncement. Hence, **it is not binding on the President;** he may follow or may not follow the opinion. However, it facilitates the government to have an authoritative legal opinion on a matter to be decided by it. **So,** **statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** The President of India has invoked this power at least 15 times since 1950, as listed below:
● **The Delhi Laws Act, 1912:** The Legislature can delegate ancillary but not essential legislative functions.
● **Kerala Education Bill, 1957:** The bill conferred unguided powers affecting minority rights under Article 30(1).
● **Berubari Case (1960):** Cession of territory requires a constitutional amendment under Article 368.
● **Sea Customs Act, 1963:** Article 289 does not exempt states from indirect Union taxes like excise.
● **Keshav Singh case (1964):** The State legislature cannot penalize High Court judges; Article 226 applies to the legislature.
● **Presidential Poll (1974):** Members of dissolved assemblies cannot vote in presidential elections; the incumbent continues till the successor takes oath.
● **The Special Courts Bill, 1978:** Parliament can validly establish Special Courts without violating judicial independence.
● **J&K Resettlement Bill, 1980:** Reference declined as the Bill had already become an Act; question was academic.
● **Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal, 1990:** State legislature cannot override Tribunal's decision; the Act was ultra vires.
● **Ismail Faruqui case (1994):** Reference on the Babri site not answered due to revived suits and lack of a dispute resolution mechanism.
● **Third Judges Case (1998):** Collegium system expanded; CJI's consultation must include a plurality of judges.
● **Natural Gas case (2001):** The State cannot legislate on natural gas; it's a Union subject under Entry 53.
● **Gujarat Assembly Dissolution (2002):** Article 174 does not constrain EC’s election schedule; applies only to functional assemblies.
● **2G/Natural Resources Allocation (2012):** Auction is not the only method for allocating natural resources across all sectors.
● **Punjab Termination of Agreement Act, 2004:** Punjab cannot unilaterally nullify water-sharing agreements validated by court decrees.
**Relevance:** Recently, the President of India has invoked the Supreme Court’s advisory jurisdiction to seek an opinion on whether timelines could be set for the President and Governors to act on Bills passed by State Assemblies.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
questions.microTopic: Amendment and Basic Structure
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act,
1992 made a provision for the mandatory creation of the Gram Sabha.
II.	 Members at all three levels of Panchayati Raj
institutions are elected directly by the people.
III. 	If the State government dissolves the
Panchayat before the end of its five-year term, fresh elections must be held within six months of such dissolution.
IV. 	 One-third of the positions in all Panchayat
institutions are reserved for women.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
In 1992, the **73rd and 74th Constitutional Amendments** were passed by the Parliament. The 73rd Amendment is about rural local governments (which are also known as Panchayati Raj Institutions or PRIs) and the 74th amendment made the provisions relating to urban local government (Nagarpalikas). The 73rd and 74th Amendments came into force in 1993.
● **Three Tier Structure:** All States have a uniform **three tier** Panchayati Raj structure. At the base is the **‘Gram Panchayat‘.** A Gram Panchayat covers a village or group of villages. The intermediary level is the Mandal (also referred to as Block or Taluka). These bodies are called Mandal or Taluka Panchayats. At the apex is the Zilla Panchayat covering the entire rural area of the District. **The amendment also** **made a provision for the mandatory creation of the Gram Sabha.** The Gram Sabha would comprise all the adult members registered as voters in the Panchayat area. Its role and functions are decided by State legislation. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Elections: All the three levels** of Panchayati Raj institutions are **elected directly by the people.** The term of each Panchayat body is five years. If the State government dissolves the Panchayat **before** **the end of its five year term**, fresh elections must be held **within six months of such dissolution.** This is an important provision that ensures the existence of elected local bodies. **So, statements II** **and III are correct.**
● **Reservations: One third** of the positions in **all panchayat institutions are reserved for women.** Reservations for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes are also provided for at all the three levels, in proportion to their population. If the States find it necessary, they can also provide for reservations for the other backward classes (OBCs). **So, statement IV is correct.** **So, all four of the above statements are correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Banking
II. 	 Public health
III. 	Post and Telegraph
IV. 	 Prison
V. 	 Cyber Laws
How many of the above are in the Union List under the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Constitution of India has divided the powers between the Centre and the states in terms of the **Union List, State List and Concurrent List in the Seventh Schedule**.
● The Union List consists of 98 subjects (originally 97), the State List 59 subjects (originally 66) and the Concurrent List 52 subjects (originally 47).
● The Union List, also known as List-I, is a part of the Seventh Schedule to the Constitution of India, outlining subjects on which the **Union Government (Parliament) has exclusive legislative** **power**. It primarily covers areas of national importance and requires uniform laws across the country.
● The Union List includes subjects like Defence**,** Atomic Energy**,** Foreign Affairs**,** War and Peace**, Banking,** Railways**, Post and Telegraph,** Airways**,** Ports**,** Foreign Trade**,** Currency & Coinage. **So, points I and** **III are correct.**
● **The State List** includes subjects like Agriculture**,** Police**, Prison,** Local Government**, Public** **Health,** Land**,** Liquor**,** Trade and Commerce**,** Livestock and Animal Husbandry and State Public Services. **So,** **points II and IV are not correct.**
● **The residuary subjects** (i.e., those which are not mentioned in any of the three lists) are given to the Centre. E.g. **Cyber laws. So, point V is not correct.**
● **Concurrent List** includes subjects like Education, Transfer of Property (other than agricultural land), Forests, Trade Unions, Adulteration, Adoption and Succession.
**So, only two of the above are mentioned in the Union List under the Seventh Schedule of the** **Constitution of India.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Emergency Provisions
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following statements is correct
regarding the federal distribution of powers in India during the National Emergency under Article 352?

options.a:
The normal distribution of the legislative
powers between the Centre and the States is not suspended
options.b:
The President assumes direct control over the
administration of all States
options.c:
The Parliament is empowered to make laws
on any subject mentioned in the State List
options.d:
The Assembly of the State is automatically
dissolved

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Under **Article 352,** the **President of India** can declare a National Emergency when the security of India or a part of it is threatened by **war or external aggression or armed rebellion.** When a National Emergency is declared on the ground of ‘war’ or ‘external aggression’, it is known as ‘External Emergency’. On the other hand, when it is declared on the ground of ‘armed rebellion’, it is known as ‘Internal Emergency.
● The President, however, can proclaim a National Emergency only after receiving a written recommendation from the Cabinet. This means that the emergency can be declared only on the concurrence of the Cabinet and not merely on the advice of the Prime Minister.
● Once an emergency is proclaimed, the federal distribution of powers remains **practically suspended** and all the powers are concentrated in the hands of the **Union Government**. The **Parliament**
**becomes empowered to make laws on any subject mentioned in the State List.** Although the legislative power of a State Legislature is not suspended, it becomes subject to the overriding power of the Parliament. Thus, the normal distribution of the legislative powers between the Centre and the States is suspended, though the State Legislatures are not suspended.
● Further, the government also gets the power to curtail or restrict all or any of the Fundamental Rights during the emergency. Constitution makers, through the wording of Article 352, make it clear that an Emergency is an extraordinary condition in which normal democratic politics cannot function. Hence, special powers are granted to the government. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.microTopic: Fundamental Rights
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Type of Writ Description
I. - Habeas corpus To release a person from unlawful deten­ tion
II. - Mandamus To restrain a subordi­ nate court from exceed­ ing its jurisdiction
III. Certiorari To quash an order passed by a lower court or tribunal
IV. - Quo Warranto To direct a public of­ ficial to perform their lawful duty
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In India, the courts can issue various special orders known as writs not only for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights but also for other purposes. Dr. Ambedkar considered the right to constitutional remedies as ‘the heart and soul of the constitution’. It is so because this right gives a citizen the right to approach a High Court or the Supreme Court to get any of the Fundamental Rights restored in case of their violation.
● The courts can issue various special orders known as writs: ○ **Habeas corpus:** A writ of habeas corpus means that the court orders that the arrested person should be presented before it. It can also be used to **set free an arrested person** if the manner or **grounds of arrest are not lawful or satisfactory. So, pair I is correctly matched.** ○ **Mandamus:** This writ is issued when the court finds that a particular office holder is **not doing** **their legal duty** and thereby is infringing on the right of an individual. **So, pair II is not** **correctly matched.**
■ **Prohibition:** This writ is issued by a higher court (High Court or Supreme Court) when
a lower court has considered a case going beyond its jurisdiction. ○ **Certiorari:** Under this writ, the court orders a lower court or another authority to **transfer** **a matter pending before it to a higher authority or court. So, pair III is correctly** **matched.** ○ **Quo Warranto:** If the court finds that a person is **holding office but is not entitled to hold** **that office,** it issues the writ of quo warranto and restricts that person from acting as an office holder. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: President, Vice President, Governor
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are judicial powers of the
Governor of a State?
I. 	 S/he is consulted by the President while
appointing the judges of the State High Court.
II. 	 S/he makes appointments, postings and
promotions of the District judges in consultation with the State High Court.
III. 	S/he can commute the sentence of anyone
convicted under a law within the State's executive jurisdiction.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **d**
**Explanation:** The Governor's judicial powers and functions are primarily centered around the power to grant pardons, reprieves, respites, or remissions of punishment and to suspend, remit, or commute sentences for any person convicted under State law. The **judicial powers and functions** of the governor are:
● S/he is consulted by the **President while appointing the judges of the concerned State High** **Court. So, statement I is correct.**
● S/he **makes appointments, postings and promotions of the District judges in consultation** **with the State High Court.**  **So, statement II is correct.**
● S/he can grant pardons, reprieves, respites and remissions of punishment or suspend, remit and **commute the sentence of any person convicted of any offence** against any law relating to a matter to which the **executive power of the State extends. So, statement III is correct.**
● S/he also appoints persons to the judicial service of the State (other than District judges) in consultation with the State High Court and the State Public Service Commission. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Preamble
questions.questionText:
“I shall strive for a Constitution which will release
India from all thralldom and patronage... I shall work for an India in which the poorest shall feel that it is their country in whose making they have an effective voice”. An India in which there shall be no high class and low class of people; an India in which all communities shall live in perfect harmony. This vision of inclusive and participatory democracy was articulated by:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**:
● **Mahatma Gandhi,** although not being a member of the Constituent Assembly, inspired the philosophy of the Indian constitution through his thoughts and writings. In 1931 while writing in his magazine **‘Young** **India’** he gave his vision for the Constitution when he wrote that **“I shall strive for a constitution**
**which will release India from all thralldom and patronage …** I shall work for an India in which the **poorest shall feel that** it is their country in whose making **they have an effective voice;** **an** **India in which there shall be no high class and low class of people; an India in which all** **communities shall live in perfect harmony**. There can be no room in such an India for the curse of untouchability or the curse of intoxicating drinks and drugs. Women will enjoy the same rights as men … I shall be satisfied with nothing else”. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
questions.questionText:
Arrange the following events in chronological
order:
I. 	 Signing of the Maastricht Treaty leading to
the formation of the European Union
II. 	 Establishment of BRICS as a formal grouping
III. 	Formation of the World Trade Organization
(WTO)
IV. 	 Brexit Referendum in the United Kingdom
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Signing of the Maastricht Treaty leading to the formation of the European Union:** The European Union is the result of the political integration of Europe, which gathered pace after the disintegration of the Soviet Union**. The European Union was founded in 1992 with the** **Maastricht Treaty.** The EU was given its current structure and powers in 2007 with the Lisbon Treaty, also known as the Reform Treaty.
● **World Trade Organization (WTO) Formation (1995):** The WTO was **established on 1** **January 1995,** replacing the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT). It serves as the primary international organization governing trade rules and dispute resolution among nations.
● **Establishment of BRICS as a formal grouping:** **BRICS began** as an informal grouping called **BRIC,** initially formulated by economist Jim O'Neill in **2001** to represent the potential of Brazil, Russia, India, and China. The formalization of **BRIC as a diplomatic grouping occurred in 2006,** with annual meetings of Foreign Ministers on the sidelines of the UN General Assembly. The first BRIC Summit was held in **Russia in 2009**, and **South Africa joined in 2010,** expanding the group to BRICS.
● **Brexit Referendum (2016):** It was held on **23 June 2016,** the United Kingdom voted to leave the European Union. This was a major political event with wide-reaching economic, diplomatic, and geopolitical consequences, especially for the EU and global trade. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
questions.questionText:
With reference to the types of goods in an
economy, consider the following pairs:
Item Category
I. - Steel sheets for manu­ facturing of cans
Intermediate good
II. - Clothes for school uniform
Consumption good
III. Factory Machines Capital good
IV. Tea leaves at restau­
rant
Final good
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In economics, goods are the items produced by firms by using factors of production in order to satisfy the needs and wants of customers. They are the end result of production. Goods provide utility when they are consumed.
● Of the total production taking place in the economy a large number of products don t end up in final consumption and are not capital goods either. Such goods may be used by other producers as material inputs. **Examples are steel sheets** used for making automobiles and copper used for making utensils. **These are intermediate goods**, mostly used as raw material or inputs for production of other commodities.**. So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Consumer goods** are finished products bought by individual buyers for their use. Also called final goods or retail goods, consumer goods are the end result of production and manufacturing. **Goods like food** **and clothing**, and services like recreation that are consumed when purchased by their ultimate consumers are called **consumption goods** or **consumer goods**. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Capital goods** are of durable character which are used in the production process. These are tools, implements and machines. While they make production of other commodities feasible, they themselves **don’t get transformed in the production process.** **They are also final goods yet they are not** **final goods to be ultimately consumed**. Thus, capital goods are any tangible asset used by a business to produce goods or services for final consumption by customers. Machines in factories are used for producing final products for consumption therefore they are capital good. **So, pair III is correctly** **matched.**
● An item that is meant for final use and will not pass through any more stages of production or transformations is called a **final good**. **Home cooked food is not sold to the market**. **However**, if the same cooking or tea brewing was done **in a restaurant where the cooked product would** **be sold to customers**, then the same items, such as **tea leaves**, would cease to be final goods and **would be counted** as inputs to which economic value addition can take place. Therefore tea leaves at restaurants would not be counted as final goods. They are an **intermediate good because they are** **used to produce a final good (brewed tea)** that is then sold to consumers. **So, pair IV is not** **correctly matched.** **So, only three of the above pairs are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Export of electronic items made by an Indian
firm
II. 	 Total stock of goods present in inventory of a
firm
III. 	Inflation
IV. 	 Total outstanding debt to foreign countries
How many of the above are flow variables?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **b**
**Explanation:**
● In economics, various **items can be categorized as either stock variables or flow variables** **based on how they are measured in relation to time**. A **stock variable** is a quantity measured at a specific point in time. It represents the amount of something that exists at a particular instant, accumulated from past flows. It does not have a time dimension.
○ **A flow variable is a quantity measured over a period of time.** It represents the rate at
which something is changing or occurring during an interval (e.g., per hour, per month, per year).
● The sales by the firm includes sales not only to domestic buyers but also to buyers abroad i.e. **exports**. Generally sales are measured on an annual basis. **Thus, all kinds of sales are flow variables**. **So,** **point I is correct.**
● A stock is a quantity which is measurable at a particular point of time. **Inventory measured at  a** **particular time** is counted as the stock variable. **So, point II is not correct.**
● The value of inflation is measured based on the value of the Consumer Price Index, which is computed in each month and measured on a scale such as the price level in the years. Thus, **inflation**  is **measured** **over a period of time**, not at a specific point in time. Therefore it is a flow variable. **So, point III is** **correct.**
● The total outstanding debt to foreign countries at the end of the year would be a stock variable. Borrowings from foreign countries represent the total outstanding amount of money owed to foreign entities at a **specific point in time**. Therefore Foreign loans are considered **stock variables**. **So,** **point IV is not correct.** **So, only two of the above are flow variables.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Other examples of stocks are:** wealth, foreign debts, loan, inventories (not change in inventories), opening stock, money supply (amount of money), population, etc.
● **Other examples of flows are:** expenditure, savings, depreciation, interest, exports, imports, change in inventories (not mere inventories), change in money supply, lending, borrowing, rent, profit, etc. because magnitude (size) of all these are measured over a period of time.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** All capital goods produced in a year add to the existing stock of capital in the economy.
**Statement II:** A part of the capital goods produced each year goes for replacement of existing capital goods.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both statement-I and statement-II are correct
and statement-II explains statement-I
options.b:
Both statement-I and statement-II are correct,
but statement-II does **not** explain statement-I
options.c:
Statement-I is correct, but statement-II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement-I is incorrect, but statement-II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The term **depreciation** refers to the reduction in or loss of quality or value of a fixed asset through wear and tear, effusion of time, obsolescence through technology and market changes or from any other cause. Depreciation takes place in case of all fixed assets with certain possible exceptions e.g. land and antiques etc, although the process may be invisible or gradual.
● All the capital goods produced in a year **do not constitute an addition to the capital stock** already existing, in an economy. **A significant part of current output of capital goods goes in** **maintaining or replacing part of the existing stock of capital goods**. This is because the already existing capital stock suffers wear and tear and needs maintenance and replacement. **So, statement I** **is not correct.**
● A part of the capital goods produced in a particular year goes for replacement of existing capital goods and is not an addition to the stock of capital goods already existing and its value needs to be subtracted from gross investment for arriving at the measure for net investment. This **deletion, which is made** **from the value of gross investment** in order to **accommodate regular wear and tear** of capital, is called **depreciation**. Thus, **depreciation is taken into account while calculating the total** **value of goods produced**. **So, statement II is correct.** **So, statement-I is not correct but statement-II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
questions.questionText:
Consider the following scenario:
A car company owned by Country X operates
a large manufacturing facility in Country Y.
To determine the income earned by the factors
of production owned by Country Y, the profits
earned by the Country X-owned car factory must
be subtracted from Country Y’s GDP.
The macroeconomic variable that accounts for
such adjustments (additions and subtractions
based on ownership of factors of production) is
known as:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Gross Domestic Product (GDP)** measures the aggregate production of final goods and services taking place within the domestic economy during a year. But the whole of it may not accrue to the citizens of the country. For example, a citizen of India working in Saudi Arabia may be earning her wage and it will be included in the Saudi Arabian GDP. Therefore various other macroeconomic measures are being used to take accurate estimates of national income.
● **Gross National Product (GNP)** is the value of products and services produced by the citizens of a country both domestically and internationally but not including income earned by foreign residents**.**
○ In the above scenario, the car company is owned by country X and utilizing the factors of production of country Y. Therefore **to calculate the GNP we must subtract the profit** **made by the car company from the total GDP of country X.** ○ **GNP** = GDP + Factor income earned by the domestic factors of production employed in the rest of the world – Factor income earned by the factors of production of the rest of the world employed in the domestic economy ○ **or GNP = GDP + Net factor income from abroad** (**Net factor income from abroad** = Factor income earned by the domestic factors of production employed in the rest of the world – Factor income earned by the factors of production of the rest of the world employed in the domestic economy).
● A part of the capital gets consumed during the year due to wear and tear. This wear and tear is called **depreciation**. Naturally, depreciation does not become part of anybody’s income. If we deduct depreciation from GNP the measure of aggregate income that we obtain is called **Net National** **Product (NNP) at market prices**. **Thus NNP** **≡** **GNP – Depreciation.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding  GDP
deflator in India:
I. 	 Unlike the Consumer Price Index (CPI)
and Wholesale Price Index (WPI), the GDP
deflator includes all domestically produced
goods and services.
II. 	 Unlike the GDP deflator, both WPI and CPI
do not adjust for consumption or behavioural
changes.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **GDP deflator**, also known as the implicit price deflator, is a measure of inflation that reflects the price change of all domestically produced goods and services in an economy.
● The **GDP deflator** captures the prices of **all domestically produced goods and services**, making it more comprehensive than the Consumer Price Index (CPI) and Wholesale Price Index (WPI), which are based on a limited fixed basket. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ **The GDP deflator uses dynamic weights** that change with production/output composition, whereas WPI and CPI use fixed weights based on a base year.
● **CPI and WPI** are constructed using **fixed weights and fixed baskets**, and thus do not adjust for **changing consumption patterns or behavior**, unlike the **GDP deflator**, which reflects real-time economic activity. **So, statement II is correct.**
○ The CPI measures the average change over time in prices paid by consumers for a fixed basket of goods and services. ○ The WPI measures the average change in the prices of goods at the wholesale level, i.e., goods traded in bulk between businesses and not directly to consumers. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
A higher level of Gross Domestic
Product (GDP) of a country does not
indicate greater well-being of its citizens.
**Statement II:**
GDP does not account for negative externalities
such as pollution.
**Statement III:**
GDP does not record non-market transactions like
household labour.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement II and Statement III are
correct, and both of them explain Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement II and Statement III are
correct, but only one of them explains
Statement I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements II and III is correct
and that explains Statement I
options.d:
Neither Statement II nor Statement III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Gross Domestic Product (GDP)** is the total **monetary value of all final goods and services** produced within a country’s borders over a specific period, usually one year. It is a **key indicator of** **economic performance**, reflecting the health, growth, and development of the economy. However, **it ignores resource depletion and income inequality**, thereby overlooking aspects of human wellbeing. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Externalities** refer to the benefits (or harms) a firm or an individual causes to another for which they are not paid (or penalised). Externalities do not have any market in which they can be bought and sold. The GDP calculation does **not account for negative externalities** such as pollution and environmental degradation. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Many activities in an economy are not evaluated in monetary terms. For example, the **domestic** **services women perform at home are not paid for**. The exchanges which take place in the informal sector without the help of money are called barter exchanges. In barter exchanges goods (or services) are directly exchanged against each other. But since **money is not being used here**, these exchanges are not registered as part of economic activity. **So, statement III is correct**.
● It is essential for measuring the size and performance of an economy and for identifying trends in economic expansion or contraction. It provides crucial insights for policy decisions, investment planning, and international comparisons. **So, both Statement II and Statement III are correct, and both of them explain Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Money (Evolution, Types, Digital Currency, Crypto, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Term Description
I. - Double coincidence of wants
Requirement in barter where both parties want what the other has
II. - Velocity of money
Number of times a unit of currency changes hands
III. - Fiat money Money that has intrinsic value
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Double coincidence of wants** is a fundamental requirement in a **barter system**, where two parties must each possess what the other wants and be willing to exchange it. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
○ For example, a person with surplus rice who wants clothing must find another person with surplus clothing who also wants rice. Such mutual needs are rare, making direct exchange difficult.
● The **velocity of circulation of money** refers to the **number of times a unit of currency changes** **hands** during a specific time period, such as a month or a year. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
○ The velocity of circulation of money indicates how **actively money is being used** to facilitate transactions in an economy.
● **Fiat money** refers to currency notes and coins that have **no intrinsic value** of their own but derive their value from the official guarantee provided by the issuing authority, such as the Reserve Bank of India (RBI). **So, pair III is not correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Financial Markets
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** High bond interest rates are associated with a lower speculative demand for money.
**Statement II:** When interest rates are high, individuals prefer investing in bonds over holding idle cash.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct
and Statement-II explains Statement-I
options.b:
Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct,
but Statement-II does not explain Statement-I
options.c:
Statement-I is correct, but Statement-II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement-I is incorrect, but Statement-II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **speculative motive** for holding money arises from **uncertainty about future interest rates** and **bond prices**. Individuals may choose to hold wealth in the form of **money** instead of **bonds** if they expect bond prices to fall in the future, which would result in **capital losses**.
● A **bond** is a financial instrument representing a promise to pay a stream of **future monetary returns**. It is issued by firms or governments to **raise loans** and is **tradable in markets**. When the **interest** **rate is very high**, everyone expects it to fall in future and hence anticipates capital gains from bondholding. Hence, **people convert their money into bonds.** Thus, speculative demand for money is low. **So, statements I and II are correct.**
● If a person expects interest rates to **rise**, they anticipate a **fall in bond prices** and thus a **capital loss** on bond holdings. In such situations, people **prefer to hold money** rather than bonds, increasing the **speculative demand for money**. **So, both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II explains Statement-I** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Cheques drawn on demand deposits
II. 	 Coins
III. 	Currency notes
How many of the above instruments are legal tenders?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In a modern economy money consists mainly of currency notes and coins issued by the monetary authority of the country. In India currency notes are issued by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), which is the monetary authority in India. However, coins are issued by the Government of India. Apart from currency notes and coins, the balance in savings, or current account deposits, held by the public in commercial banks is also considered money since cheques drawn on these accounts are used to settle transactions. **Such deposits are called demand deposits as they are payable by the bank on** **demand from the account holder**. Other deposits, e.g. fixed deposits, have a fixed period to maturity and are referred to as time deposits.
● While demand deposits are widely accepted in practice, they are **not legal tender**. **No one is legally** **bound to accept cheques drawn on demand deposits** as a form of payment. **Currency notes** **and coins** are **legal tender**, meaning they **must be accepted by law** for the settlement of transactions.  **So, point I is not correct and points II and III are correct.** **So, only two of the above instruments are legal tenders.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
questions.questionText:
With reference to banking, consider the following
statements:
I. 	 The Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR) includes
the percentage of Net Demand and Time Liabilities (NDTL) that must be maintained in cash with the Reserve Bank of India.
II. 	 Statutory Liquidity Ratio (SLR) includes
reserves in the form of liquid assets such as cash, gold, or government securities.
III. 	A decrease in the bank rate leads to cheaper
loans by the banks.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Reserve Bank of India (RBI), as the central bank of the country, plays a key role in regulating the money supply in the economy through various tools.
● **Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR)**: It refers to the percentage of a bank's total deposits that it must hold as reserves in the form of cash with the RBI. **This amount is not available for lending or investment** **and thus acts as a brake on the credit-creation capacity of banks**. It ensures liquidity, safeguards depositors' interests, and controls inflationary tendencies by limiting the money supply.
○ **CRR** = Percentage of Net Demand and Time Liabilities (NDTL) that must be maintained in cash with the RBI. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Statutory Liquidity Ratio (SLR)**: In addition to CRR, **banks are also required to maintain a** **certain percentage of their NDTL** in the form of liquid assets such as **cash, gold, or approved** **government securities**. This requirement is known as the Statutory Liquidity Ratio (SLR). SLR helps ensure the bank's solvency and further restricts the extent to which it can use its deposits for lending purposes. **So, statement II is correct.**
○ **SLR** = Percentage of NDTL to be maintained in liquid assets with the bank itself.
● **The Bank Rate:** It is the interest rate at which the **RBI lends to commercial banks without any** **collateral**. By changing this rate, the RBI can influence the money supply in the economy. When the Bank Rate is decreased, **borrowing from the RBI becomes less expensive** for commercial banks, leading them to increase borrowing. This increases their lending capacity, which in turn increases the money supply. **So, statement III is correct.** **So,  all three of the statements given above are correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** According to a recent report by the State Bank of India (SBI), the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) could start using the Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR) as a regulatory intervention tool rather than just a liquidity management mechanism in the future.
**Q31**.
**Answer:** **c**
**Explanation:**
● Commercial banks are a part of the money-creating system of the economy. They accept deposits from the public and lend out part of these funds to those who want to borrow. They charge some rate of interest in lieu of their service.
● **Spread:** Commercial banks accept deposits from the public and lend a portion of these funds to borrowers. The interest rate charged on loans is typically higher than the interest rate paid on deposits. **The difference between these two rates is known as the spread**. This spread represents the gross income or profit margin of the bank, which is used to cover operational costs and earn profit. **So,** **pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Liquidity Trap:** It is a situation when expansionary monetary policy (increase in money supply) does not increase the interest rate, income and hence **does not stimulate economic growth**. If the **central bank tries to inject additional money into the economy**, it merely increases people's cash holdings, without affecting investment or interest rates. As a result, monetary policy becomes ineffective, since the injected liquidity does not stimulate economic activity. **So, pair II is correctly** **matched.**
● **Sterilisation:** It refers to the **actions taken by a central bank (RBI) to neutralise the impact of** **foreign exchange inflows or outflows on the domestic money supply**. For instance, if there is a large capital inflow, the RBI might buy foreign currency to prevent the appreciation of the rupee. However, this also increases the domestic money supply. To counteract this increase, the RBI may sell government securities, thereby absorbing the excess liquidity. **So, pair III is correctly matched.** **So, all three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The recent focus of the RBI in liquidity management has been on making the transmission of rate cuts faster and more effective.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Term Description/Impact
I. - Spread Difference between the interest rate paid to depositors and charged from borrowers
II. - Liquidity Trap
Injection of liquidity by the Central Bank does not stimulate economic activity
III. Sterilisation Sale or purchase of government securities to offset foreign capital flows
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Money (Evolution, Types, Digital Currency, Crypto, etc.)
questions.questionText:
In an economy, why is the total amount of money
stock in the economy much greater than the volume of high-powered money?

options.a:
The government prints more currency notes
than required
options.b:
The banks generally lend the entire amount
while keeping very less reserves
options.c:
The commercial banks create money through
the credit multiplier mechanism
options.d:
The central bank directly controls all forms
of money in circulation

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **c**
**Explanation:**
● **The total money stock in the economy,** which includes currency with the public and demand deposits with banks, is **much greater than the volume of high-powered money** (also known as reserve money or base money). This is because high-powered money acts as a **foundation upon which** **a much larger amount of money is created through the banking system**. While the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) issues base money, the broader money supply expands due to the functioning of commercial banks.
● The money multiplier refers to the process by which banks create money through lending. When banks receive deposits, they keep a fraction as reserves (as required by CRR and SLR) and lend out the rest. **The money lent is redeposited in banks, allowing further lending**. This cycle continues, and as a result, a multiple of the initial reserves is created in the form of new money. This mechanism explains how banks significantly increase the money supply beyond the base money.
● The money multiplier effect arises due to fractional reserve banking, where banks lend a portion of deposits, and this process repeats, expanding the total money supply far beyond the base or highpowered money. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** According to recent discussions post Budget 2025, it is said that tax benefits and money multiplier effect could boost consumption in India.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Which of the following best explains the Paradox
of Thrift in macroeconomics?

options.a:
Households start spending more due to
increase in household income
options.b:
People cut back on spending and increase
savings during a recession
options.c:
Increased government spending despite
lower revenues
options.d:
Decrease in private investments due to rising
interest rates on borrowings

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **b**
**Explanation:**
● The **Paradox of Thrift** means that while saving money is a smart move for an individual during tough times, if **everyone starts saving more and spending less during a recession, it can actually** **make the economy worse**.
● As per Keynesian economics, during a recession, many resources like workers and machines are not fully used. To fix this, people need to spend more so that demand increases. But if everyone cuts down on spending, businesses earn less, invest less, and may lay off workers. This reduces overall income in the economy. So, even though **people try to save more, they may end up saving less because** **their incomes fall—this is the paradox**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A recent report indicates that India's household savings are plummeting, impacting the economy and individual financial security.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Balance of Payments (BoP)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Indian economy, which
of the following factors are included in Capital Expenditure?
I. 	 Expenditure on construction of roads
II. 	 Loans granted to States and Union Territories
III. 	Repayment of government borrowings
IV. 	 Interest payments on government loans
V. 	 Regular payments to government employees
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **a**
**Explanation:**
● **Capital Expenditure** refers to government spending that leads to the creation of long-term assets or a reduction in financial liabilities. It **includes:**
○ **Investment in infrastructure such as roads,** buildings, and machinery, as well as acquisition of land, shares, and **loans or advances to States, Union Territories, and public sector** **undertakings**. These expenditures are meant to enhance the productive capacity of the economy and yield benefits over a longer period. **So points I and II are correct.** ○ The **repayment of government borrowings**, specifically the principal amount, is considered a capital expenditure. This is because it reduces the government's liabilities, which is a characteristic of capital expenditure. **So, point III is correct.**
● On the other hand, **Revenue Expenditure includes the routine and recurring expenses of the** **government that do not result in asset creation or liability reduction.** These are necessary for the day-to-day functioning of the administration and delivery of public services. Examples include salaries, pensions, subsidies, interest payments on loans, and grants to state governments. **So points IV and V** **are not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A recent report by ICRA says that the government has scope to increase the total capital expenditure (capex) of the Indian economy.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following is correct regarding
the Ricardian Equivalence Proposition in macroeconomics?

options.a:
Taxation and borrowing are equivalent means
of financing expenditure
options.b:
Increased government borrowing leads to
higher interest rates and crowds out private investment
options.c:
Fiscal deficits always result in inflationary
pressure in the economy
options.d:
Government spending is always more
effective than tax cuts in stimulating economic activity

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ricardian equivalence** is an economic theory that says that **financing government spending out** **of current taxes or future taxes** (and current deficits) will have equivalent effects on the overall economy. This means that attempts to stimulate an economy by increasing debt-financed government spending will **not be effective because investors and consumers understand that the debt will** **eventually have to be paid for in the form of future taxes**.
● This theory has been widely interpreted as undermining the Keynesian notion that deficit spending can boost economic performance, even in the short run. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Monetary Policy
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are most likely
consequences of the reduction in Repo rate:
I. 	 Decrease in returns for the retail investors in
the stock market
II. 	 Reduction in inflation rates in the markets
III. 	Decreased interest rates for the borrowers
IV. 	 Increase in money supply in the economy
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Repo Rate** is a component of the **Liquidity Adjustment Facility** of the Reserve Bank of India (RBI). It is the rate at which the **RBI lends short-term money to commercial banks.** It is a key tool in the RBI’s monetary policy toolkit to regulate liquidity, inflation, and overall credit availability.
● A reduction in the Repo rate generally encourages borrowing and investment, including in the stock market. This can boost stock prices and potentially improve returns for retail investors. **The change** **in repo rates is not directly linked to reducing returns in the stock markets**. In fact, lower interest rates often shift investments from fixed income to equities. **So, point I is not correct.**
● When the central bank cuts the rate, borrowing becomes cheaper, thus increasing the money supply. This may lead to **higher inflation rates in the economy.** Rate cuts are usually anti-deflationary, not anti-inflationary. **So, point II is not correct.**
● When the Repo rate is reduced, banks can borrow more cheaply from the RBI. This usually **results in** **lower lending rates for consumers and businesses, encouraging more loans.** This is a key tool used to stimulate borrowing and investment. **So, point III is correct.**
● Lower Repo rates lead to cheaper loans, which often result in more credit being extended by banks. This increases liquidity and the overall money supply in the economy. **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
If the real exchange rate between two countries
