questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Rajatarangini, written by Kalhana, records
the history of Kashmir’s kings.
II. 	 Kitab ul-Hind is a Persian text by Al-Biruni
which records the rule of Mahmud of Ghazni.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In medieval India, books, chronicles and pillar inscriptions (prashastis) were used to give detail about administration. One such initiative was **Rajatarangini, a long Sanskrit poem containing the** **history of kings who ruled over Kashmir. I**t was composed by an author named **Kalhana**. He used a variety of sources, including inscriptions, documents, eyewitness accounts and earlier histories, to write his account. Unlike the writers of prashastis, he was **often critical about rulers and their policies**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni, Afghanistan** ruled from 997 to 1030 AD, and extended control over parts of Central Asia, Iran and the north-western part of the subcontinent. He raided the Indian subcontinent almost every year – his targets were wealthy temples, including that of Somnath, Gujarat. Much of the wealth Mahmud carried away was used to create a splendid capital city at Ghazni.
○ He was also interested in finding out more about the people he conquered, and entrusted a scholar named **Al-Biruni** to write an account of the subcontinent. This **Arabic work, known** **as the Kitab ul-Hind**, remains an important source for historians. He consulted Sanskrit scholars to prepare this account. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● In 1236, Sultan Iltutmish’s daughter, **Raziyya**, became Sultan. The chronicler of the age, Minhaj-i Siraj, recognised that she was more able and qualified than all her brothers. But he was not comfortable at having a queen as ruler. Nor were the nobles happy at her attempts to rule independently. She was removed from the throne in 1240. **Minhaj-i Siraj thought that the queen’s rule went against the** **ideal social order created by God**, in which women were supposed to be subordinate to men. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Both Buddha and Mahavir preached that
asceticism and penance are required for
salvation.
II. 	 Both Buddha and Mahavir preached their
teachings in Pali.
III. Both Buddhism and Jainism believe in the
theory of Karma.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Buddha (567- 487 B.C.) belonged to a small gana known as the **Sakya gana**, and was a kshatriya. When he was a young man, he left the comforts of his home in search of knowledge. He wandered for several years, meeting and holding discussions with other thinkers. He finally decided to find his own path to realisation, and meditated for days under a peepal tree at Bodh Gaya in Bihar, where he attained enlightenment. After that, **he was known as the Buddha or the ‘Wise One’**.
○ In Jainism, **"Jina" means "conqueror"** or "victor," specifically referring to someone who has overcome worldly passions and desires. The term is used for Jain Tirthankaras, who are considered to have achieved enlightenment and liberation. The word "Jain" itself is derived from "Jina," meaning a follower of the Jina.
● According to **Jaina teachings**, the cycle of birth and rebirth is shaped through **karma**. **Asceticism** **and penance are required to free oneself from the cycle of karma**. This can be achieved only by renouncing the world; therefore, monastic existence is a necessary condition of salvation.
○ **Buddha taught a "middle path" that avoided the extremes of both self-indulgence** **and severe asceticism**. He believed in a more moderate approach to self-discipline and spiritual practice, ultimately leading to enlightenment (Nirvana). **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Buddha believed that the results of our actions (called karma), whether good or bad, affect** **us both in this life and the next**. Thus, in both Jainism and Buddhism, karma plays a crucial role in the cycle of rebirth and spiritual development.
○ However, **they differ in how karma is understood.** In Jainism, karma is seen as a subtle form of matter that binds to the soul and affects its future lives. Buddhism, on the other hand, views karma as a non-physical force generated by intentional actions, impacting both the present and future, rather than a substance that binds to the soul. **So, statement III is correct.**
● The Buddha taught in the language of the ordinary people, **Prakrit**, so that everybody could understand his message. Likewise, ordinary people could understand the teachings of Mahavira and his followers, because they used **Prakrit**. There were several forms of Prakrit, used in different parts of the country, and named after the regions in which they were used. For example, the Prakrit spoken in Magadha was known as Magadhi. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
In the context of schools of Indian Philosophy and
their propounders, which one of the following is an odd one out?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Over centuries, India’s intellectual exploration of truth has come to be represented by six systems of philosophy. These are known as **Vaishesika, Nyaya, Samkhya, Yoga, Purva Mimansa and Vedanta or Uttara** **Mimansa**. These philosophies still guide scholarly discourse in the country. These six systems of philosophy are said to have been founded by sages.
● **Vaisheshika, founded by Kanada**, focuses on the categorization of reality and the nature of substances.
● **Gotama founded Nyaya philosophy**. It is primarily concerned with logic, epistemology, and the means of acquiring valid knowledge.
● **Samkhya, founded by Kapila**, posits two fundamental realities: Purusha (consciousness or spirit) and Prakriti (nature or matter). It views the world as composed of these two elements, with Purusha being the eternal, unchanging witness to Prakriti's transformations.
● **Patanjali founded Yoga** that emphasizes the interconnectedness of mind, body, and spirit, aiming for union with the divine and achieving liberation from suffering.
● **Jaimini founded Purva Mimansa** that focuses on the interpretation of the Vedic scriptures, particularly the ritualistic portions (mantra and brahmana). It emphasizes the importance of Vedic rituals (yajnas) and the concept of dharma (duty, righteousness) in achieving liberation (moksha).
● **Vyasa founded Vedanta or Uttara Mimansa**. It focuses on the philosophical content of the Upanishads (the concluding sections of the Vedas). It centers on understanding the nature of reality and the self, particularly the relationship between Brahman (the ultimate reality) and Atman (the individual self). **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement-I:** The Permanent Settlement system did not lead to a steady return on investment. **Statement-II:** The revenue demand was pegged at a high rate when the prices of agricultural produce were depressed. **Statement III:** The Sunset Law led to frequent auctioning of the zamindari in case of failed payments.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement–II and Statement–III are
correct and both of them explain Statement–I
options.b:
Both Statement–II and Statement–III are
correct, but only one of them explains Statement–I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements–II and III is
correct and that explains Statement–I
options.d:
Neither Statement–II nor Statement–III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Permanent Settlement**, also known as the Zamindari System, was a land revenue system introduced by the British East India Company in 1793 under Governor-General **Lord Cornwallis**. It aimed to fix land revenue and create a hereditary class of landlords (Zamindars) in **Bengal, Bihar and** **Orissa.**
● Company officials felt that a fixed revenue demand would give zamindars a sense of security and, **assured** **of returns on their investment**, encourage them to improve their estates. In the early decades after the Permanent Settlement, however, **zamindars regularly failed to pay the revenue demand and** **unpaid balances accumulated**. The reasons for this failure were various. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● **First:** the initial demands were very high. This was because it was felt that if the demand was fixed for all time to come, the Company would never be able to claim a share of increased income from land when prices rose and cultivation expanded. To minimise this anticipated loss, the **Company pegged** **the revenue demand high**, arguing that the burden on zamindars would gradually decline as agricultural production expanded and prices rose.
● **Second:** this high demand was imposed in the 1790s, a time when the **prices of agricultural produce** **were depressed**, making it difficult for the ryots to pay their dues to the zamindar. If the zamindar could not collect the rent, it was difficult for him to pay the Company. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Third:** The revenue was invariable, regardless of the harvest, and had to be paid punctually. In fact, according to the **Sunset Law**, if payment did not come in by sunset of the specified date, the zamindari was liable to be auctioned. **So, statement III is correct.**
● **Fourth:** The Permanent Settlement initially **limited the power of the zamindar to collect rent** **from the ryot** and manage his zamindari. **So, both Statement–II and Statement–III are correct and both of them explain Statement–I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**What was the British intention behind the introduction of the Permanent Land Revenue** **Settlement?**
● In introducing the Permanent Settlement, British officials hoped to **resolve the** **problems they had been facing since the conquest of Bengal.** By the 1770s, the rural economy in Bengal was in crisis, with recurrent famines and declining agricultural output. Officials felt that agriculture, trade and the revenue resources of the state could all be developed by encouraging investment in agriculture.
● This could be done by **securing rights of property and permanently fixing the** **revenue demand**. If the revenue demand of the state was permanently fixed, then the Company could look forward to a regular flow of revenue, while entrepreneurs could feel sure of earning a profit from their investment, since the state would not siphon it off by increasing its claim.

questions.questionText:
In the context of British India, the Fifth Report of
1813 was associated with:

options.a:
A detail account of social evils like sati
prevalent in India
options.b:
Revenue and judicial administration of Bengal
and Madras
options.c:
The account of the East India Company's trade
monopoly in India
options.d:
Status of literary and scientific knowledge in
India

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The British Parliament passed a series of Acts in the late eighteenth century **to regulate and control** **Company rule in India**. It forced the Company to produce regular reports on the administration of India and appointed committees to enquire into the affairs of the Company. The **Fifth Report** was one such report produced by a **Select Committee**. It became the basis of intense parliamentary debates on the nature of the East India Company’s rule in India. This report, along with subsequent parliamentary debates, significantly influenced the **Charter Act of 1813**.
● It ran into 1002 pages, of which over 800 pages were appendices that reproduced petitions of zamindars and ryots, reports of collectors from different districts, statistical tables on revenue returns, and **notes** **on the revenue and judicial administration of Bengal and Madras** (Tamil Nadu) written by officials.
● Researchers have carefully examined the archives of various Bengal zamindars and the local records of the districts to write about the history of colonial rule in rural Bengal. They indicate that, intent on criticising the maladministration of the company, the Fifth Report **exaggerated the collapse of** **traditional zamindari power**, as also overestimated the scale on which zamindars were losing their land. As we have seen, even when zamindaris were auctioned, zamindars were not always displaced, given the ingenious methods they used to retain their zamindaris. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With reference to the British India, consider the
following pairs:
Personality Associated with
I. - Francis Buchanan
Calcutta Alipore Zoo and Botanical Gardens
II. - William Hodges
Establishment of Ryotwari Settlement
III. Robert
Merttins Bird
Development of Mahalwari System
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Francis Buchanan** was a physician who came to India and served in the Bengal Medical Service (from 1794 to 1815). For a few years he was surgeon to the Governor-General of India, Lord Wellesley. During his stay in Calcutta (Kolkata), he organised a zoo that became the **Calcutta Alipore Zoo**; he was also in charge of the **Botanical Gardens** for a short period. On the request of the Government of Bengal, he undertook detailed **surveys of the areas under the jurisdiction of the British East India** **Company**.  **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **William Hodges** was a **British artist** who accompanied Captain Cook on his second voyage to the Pacific (1772-75), and then came to India. In 1781 he became a friend of Augustus Cleveland, the Collector of Bhagalpur. On the invitation of Cleveland, Hodges accompanied him to the Jangal Mahals in 1782, and painted a set of aquatints. Like many other British painters of the time, Hodges searched for the picturesque. Artists in search of the picturesque were inspired by the ideals of Romanticism, a tradition of thought that celebrated nature and admired its magnificence and power. **So, pair II is not** **correctly matched.**
● **The Mahalwari System** was one of three major land revenue systems established by the British in India in the nineteenth century. It combined elements of the Zamindari and Ryotwari systems to overcome their limitations. It aimed to create a more flexible and community-based approach to land revenue collection. The Mahalwari System began to be considered for implementation in parts of Northern India around 1819, following a **recommendation by Holt Mackenzie,** the secretary to the board of commissioners. His proposal was formalised through **Regulation VII of 1822. The** **Regulation of 1833** aimed to simplify the process of estimating land production. **Robert Merttins** **Bird, known as the Father of Land Settlements in Northern India,** oversaw this revised scheme,
which involved surveying tracts of land to delineate field boundaries for both cultivated and fallow areas. **So, pair III is correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Revolt of 1857, consider the
following pairs:
Leader Region
I. - Maulvi Ahmadullah Shah Faizabad
II. - Shah Mal Lucknow
III. Gonoo Chotanagpur
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Revolt of 1857**, India's first major resistance against British rule, sparked widespread uprisings. It had political, economic and social causes. Despite failure, it marked a turning point in India's struggle for independence.
● **Maulvi Ahmadullah Shah** was one of the many maulvis who played an important part in the Revolt of 1857. In 1856, he was seen moving from village to village preaching jehad (religious war) against the British and urging people to rebel. He moved in a palanquin, with drumbeaters in front and followers at the rear. He was therefore popularly called **Danka Shah** – the maulvi with the drum (danka). He **made** **Faizabad his centre** and spearheaded his revolt in all of Awadh. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Shah Mal** lived in a large village in **pargana Barout in** **Uttar Pradesh (currently in Baghpat district)**. He belonged to a clan of Jat cultivators whose kinship ties extended over chaurasee des (eighty-four villages). The lands in the region were irrigated and fertile, with rich dark loam soil. Many of the cultivators were losing land to outsiders, to traders and moneylenders who were coming into the area. Shah Mal mobilised the headmen and cultivators of chaurasee des, moving at night from village to village, urging people to rebel against the British. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
●
● Elsewhere, local leaders emerged, urging peasants, zamindars and tribals to revolt. **Gonoo, a tribal** **cultivator of Singhbhum in Chotanagpur, became** **a rebel leader of the Kol tribals of the region.** Being an extremely popular leader among his tribe, he encouraged the tribals to join forces with him and fight against the British. Singhbhum was located to the South of Porahat and came under the authority of the Raja of Porahat. **Gonoo declared his loyalty** **to the Raja of Porahat. So, pair III is correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Who were other key leaders of the Revolt of 1857?**
● **Mangal Pandey** was one of the first to ignite the flames of rebellion. Refusing to use the new Enfield rifle cartridges—which were rumoured to be greased with pig and cow fat, a young sepoy of the 34th Native Infantry, Mangal Pandey fired at the sergeant major of his unit at Barrackpore.
● **Nana Saheb**, the adopted son of the exiled Maratha Peshwa Baji Rao II, emerged as a significant leader in Kanpur. In June 1857, he attacked the British soldiers of the 53rd Native Infantry alongside **Tantia Tope**, targeting the British East India Company’s entrenchment under General Sir Hugh Wheeler.
● **Begum Hazrat Mahal** was the wife of the Nawab of Awadh and a prominent leader of the revolt in Lucknow. She courageously took charge after her husband’s exile, proclaiming her son, Birjis Kadr, as the Nawab of Awadh.
● **Rani Laxmibai** emerged as one of the most remarkable leaders of the revolt, taking charge of the sepoys in Jhansi when Lord Dalhousie, the Governor-General, refused to recognize her adopted son as the rightful heir and annexed the state using the controversial ‘Doctrine of Lapse’.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part I: The Union and its territory
questions.microTopic: Union, States and their Territory, UTs
questions.questionText:
In British India, who was referred to as a
‘Resident’?

options.a:
A British officer in charge of administering
directly ruled provinces
options.b:
An agent of the East India Company
responsible for revenue administration
options.c:
A member of the British Parliament posted in
India
options.d:
A local official acting as intermediary between
British authorities and Indian rulers

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Resident** was the designation of a representative of the Governor General who lived in a State which was not under direct British rule.
**Points To Remember** **“The Hall of Justice” by Shah Mal**
● Locally acknowledged as the Raja, Shah Mal took over the bungalow of an English officer, turned it into a **“hall of justice”**, settling disputes and dispensing judgments. He also set up an amazingly effective network of intelligence.
● For a period the people of the area felt that firangi raj was over, and their raj had come. Shah Mal was killed in battle in July 1857.
● The resident was a senior British official stationed in the **capital of a princely state**, acting as both a diplomat and a political advisor. They were responsible for overseeing the state's governance and ensuring its adherence to the British system of indirect rule through the **Subsidiary Alliance**. This was seen as a system of indirect rule that was carefully controlled by the British Resident. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Subsidiary Alliance**
● It was a system devised by **Lord Wellesley in 1798**. All those who entered into such an alliance with the British had to accept certain terms and conditions:
○ The British would be responsible for protecting their ally from external and internal threats to their power. ○ In the territory of the ally, a British armed contingent would be stationed. ○ The ally would have to provide the resources for maintaining this contingent. ○ The ally could enter into agreements with other rulers or engage in warfare only with the permission of the British.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding
Mahatma Gandhi’s first major public appearance
at the opening of the Banaras Hindu University:
I. 	 He considered that Indian nationalism was
an elite phenomenon, a creation of lawyers,
doctors and landlords.
II. 	 The first public announcement of Gandhi's
desire was to make Indian nationalism more
properly representative of the Indian people
as a whole.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The India that **Mahatma Gandhi** came back to in 1915 was rather different from the one that he had left in 1893. Although still a colony of the British, it was far **more active in a political sense**. The Indian National Congress now had branches in most major cities and towns.
● On Gokhale’s advice, **Gandhiji spent a year travelling around British India**, getting to know the land and its peoples. His **first major public appearance was at the opening of the Banaras Hindu** **University (BHU)** in February 1916.
● When his turn came to speak, Gandhiji charged the Indian elite with a lack of concern for the labouring poor. The opening of the BHU, he said, was **“certainly a most gorgeous show”**. But he worried about the contrast between the **“richly bedecked noblemen”** present and **“millions of the poor”** Indians who were absent.
○ Gandhiji told the privileged invitees that **“there is no salvation for India unless you strip** **yourself of this jewellery and hold it in trust for your countrymen in India”**. “There can be no spirit of self-government about us,” he went on, “if we take away or allow others to take away from the peasants almost the whole of the results of their labour. Our salvation can only come through the farmer. Neither the lawyers, nor the doctors, nor the rich landlords are going to secure it.”
● Thus, Gandhiji’s speech at Banaras in February 1916 was, at one level, merely a statement of fact – namely, that **Indian nationalism was an elite phenomenon, a creation of lawyers and doctors** **and landlords.** But, at another level, it was also a statement of intent – the first public announcement of Gandhiji’s own **desire to make Indian nationalism more properly representative of the** **Indian people as a whole.** **So, both statements I and II are correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement-I:**
Charkha became a symbol of the Indian national
movement for the first time under Mahatma
Gandhi.
**Statement-II:**
Charkha was seen as a symbol that could provide
the poor with supplementary income and make
them self-reliant.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct
and Statement–II explains Statement–I
options.b:
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are
correct, but Statement–II does **not** explain
Statement–I
options.c:
Statement–I is correct, but Statement–II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement–I is incorrect, but Statement–II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Mahatma Gandhi** was profoundly critical of the modern age in which machines enslaved humans and displaced labour. He saw the charkha as a symbol of a human society that would not glorify machines and technology. The spinning wheel, moreover, **could provide the poor with supplementary** **income and make them self-reliant**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● In 1905, the **charkha became a symbol of the Swadeshi movement** - a part of the Indian Independence movement aimed at disbanding the British Empire by making the citizens of India more self-reliant. While the previous attempt at a Swadeshi movement was confined to the urban centre alone, the message of the new wave started by Gandhi reached the rural masses as well. **So, statement I is** **not correct.** **So, Statement–I is incorrect, but Statement–II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Money (Evolution, Types, Digital Currency, Crypto, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Grazing food chain doesn’t consists of
microbes or any other decomposers.
II. 	 The sun is the main source of energy for the
detritus food chain.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The transfer of food energy through a sequence of eating and being eaten by the organisms in an ecosystem is termed as **Food Chain**.
● **Food chains are of two basic types:** o **Grazing Food chain:** It **starts from the green plants**, the major source of energy
for this chain is taken from the sun as plants carry out the process of photosynthesis in presence of sunlight. The green plants are the primary producer and eaten up by herbivores, which in turn are eaten up by carnivores. This food chain doesn’t consist of microbes or other decomposers; it is carried out by the macroscopic organisms. **So,** **statement I is correct.** o **Detritus Food Chain:** The **detritus food chain starts from the dead organic**
**matter such as dead bodies of animals or fallen leaves**, which are eaten by microorganisms and then followed by detritus feeding organisms (detritivores) and their predators. This food chain has the remains of detritus as the major source of energy, and this process gets completed by the **subsoil organisms,** which can either be macroscopic or microscopic. Thus, these **food chains are less dependent on direct solar** **energy. So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Functionally, the distinction between the two is the time lag between the direct** **consumption of living plants and ultimate utilization of dead organic matter.** The importance of two food chains may differ in different ecosystems, in some cases grazing is more important and in others, detritus is more important.
● In a **terrestrial ecosystem,** a **huge quantity of biomass** is produced in a unit time, that **cannot be** **all consumed by herbivores** and a large part of it enters into the detritus compartment in the form of litter. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Trophic Levels and Energy Flow:**
● Based on the **source of their nutrition or food, organisms occupy a specific place** in the food chain that is known as their **trophic level.**
● **Producers belong to the first trophic level**, herbivores (primary consumer) to the second and carnivores (secondary consumer) to the third.
● The **amount of energy passed from each level decreases at successive trophic** **levels**. When any organism dies, it is converted to detritus or dead biomass that serves as an energy source for decomposers.
● Organisms at each trophic level depend on those at the lower trophic level for their energy demands.
● Each trophic level has a **certain mass of living material at a particular time called** **the standing crop**. The standing crop is measured as the mass of living organisms (biomass) or the number in a unit area.
● The number of trophic levels in the grazing food chain is restricted as the transfer of energy follows 10 per cent law – **only 10 per cent of the energy is transferred to each** **trophic level from the lower trophic level.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Oceanic rocks are the major reservoirs of
phosphorus.
II. 	 The atmospheric input of phosphorus through
rainfall is much more than that of carbon.
III. 	Gaseous exchange of phosphorus between the
organism and the environment is negligible.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Phosphorus,** an essential element for living cells, is present in different soluble and adsorbed chemical forms found in soil, sediment and water.
● Phosphorus is a component of bones, teeth and the genetic material. In the form of phospholipids, phosphorus is also a component of cell membrane structure and of the body’s key energy source, Adenosine triphosphate (ATP).
● The phosphorus cycle is a type of **sedimentary nutrient cycle**, unlike the carbon and nitrogen cycles
which are gaseous. It is called a sedimentary cycle because the major reservoir of phosphorus is in the rock form, particularly **oceanic and terrestrial rocks.** Weathering of these rocks makes phosphorus available to ecosystems. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **atmospheric input of** **phosphorus is minimal**, as phosphorus does not circulate through the atmosphere in a gaseous state like carbon or nitrogen. **Rainfall contributes very little phosphorus** **compared to the vast input of carbon** (mainly as CO₂), this becomes one of the major reasons for **low turnover rate** of the Phosphorous molecules. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● The **phosphorus cycle lacks a significant gaseous phase**, so gaseous exchange between organisms and the **environment is negligible** and the phosphorous stored in the bodies of organisms (bones and teeth are the major reservoirs of phosphorous in the body) are made available to the environment in a reduced manner. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following statements best
describes the term 'brood parasitism'?

options.a:
Takeover of the replication machinery of
the host by retroviruses to generate its own
copies of DNA.
options.b:
Practice of laying eggs in the nests of other
species of birds for incubation.
options.c:
The parasite is attached to the plant but
doesn’t take any nutrients from the host.
options.d:
Extreme form of parasitism, wherein the
host dies of infection and the parasite then becomes sarcophagic and feeds on the dead body of the host.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Brood parasitism,** or the **laying of one's eggs in the nest of another individual,** is a reproductive strategy whereby parasites foist the cost of rearing their offspring onto another individual, the host.
● **Brood parasitism may be facultative at the species or individual levels,** with some eggs incubated by the mother and others laid in foreign nests, or obligate. Brood parasitism may also be intraspecific, with eggs laid in other nests of the parasite's own species, or interspecific, with all eggs laid in the nests of other species.
● For the parasite, benefits include **increased fecundity due to greater allocation of resources** toward mating and producing more eggs rather than defending nests, incubating eggs, and feeding young.
● **For hosts of brood parasitic birds, the costs of parasitism range from diminished nestling** **growth rate**, due to competition with larger and more competitive parasitic offspring (cowbirds, whydahs), to total loss of breeding by the abandonment of parasitized broods (cowbirds, cuckoos), the eviction of all host eggs by the early-hatching parasites (cuckoos), or the killing of host hatchlings by parasitic hatchlings (cuckoos, honeyguides) **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding
Humus:
I. 	 It has a high water-holding capacity and helps
nutrient retention in the soil.
II. 	 It is highly resistant to microbial action and
undergoes decomposition at an extremely
slow rate.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Humus** is a **dark-coloured organic substance formed in the soil during the final stages of** **decomposition of plant and animal matter**. It plays an essential role in soil fertility and structure.
● The process of humus formation is known as **Humification,** which is defined as the conversion of organic material to soil organic matter. **Mineralisation and humification are the two processes** **taking place during decomposition.**
● The humus is **highly resistant to microbial action and undergoes decomposition at an** **extremely slow rate**, acting as a reservoir of nutrients in the soil. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Humus has a **colloidal structure**, which gives it a **high water-holding capacity and helps in** **nutrient retention in the soil**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The humus is **further degraded by some microbes and release of inorganic nutrients occurs** **by the process known as mineralisation**. Hence, it is not the end product of the decomposition process. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Plants have a greater species diversity than
animals.
II. 	 Arthropods have the maximum number of
species in the animal kingdom.
III. 	Amongst animals Invertebrates are more
numerous than vertebrates.
IV. 	 Fungi have more species recorded than all
vertebrate groups combined.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The total number of described species globally is about 1.7–1.8 million. Among these, **animals** **dominate in terms of diversity and numbers**, with specific groups contributing heavily to the total count.
● While plant diversity is vast, animal species far outnumber plant species. **Animals account for over** **70% of the recorded species**, whereas plants represent only about 22%. **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● Among animals, **arthropods, especially** **insects, are the most diverse,** with over 1 million species recorded. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● **Invertebrates, including arthropods,** **molluscs,** **and** **annelids,** **far** **outnumber vertebrates**, both in terms of species and population. **So, statement** **III is correct.**
● **Fungi have more species recorded (about 70,000) than all vertebrate groups combined** (fishes, amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals). **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Balance of Payments (BoP)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Ecological Interaction
Example
I. - Predation Biological control methods in agriculture for pest control
II. - Commensalism Orchids growing on Mango plants as an epiphyte
III. - Mutualism Association of fungus with roots of plants to form Mycorrhizae
IV. - Ammensalism Antibiotic production by fungus to prevent the growth of other microorganisms
Which of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In any natural ecosystem, organisms interact in various ways forming a **biological community** in such a way that **no species exists in isolation**. These interactions can be **beneficial (+), detrimental (–** **) or neutral (0)** to each other.
● When two species interact, the **effect on each species** is used to classify the interaction. Some of the different types of interactions include,
● **Predation (+, –):** o Predation is an ecological interaction in which one species, known as the **predator, kills**
**and consumes another species, called the prey**. o This relationship is crucial for **maintaining the balance and stability of ecosystems** as
it transfers energy to higher trophic levels, regulates prey population and prevents ecosystem destabilization due to overpopulation of prey. o **Examples:** Tiger hunting a deer; Sparrow eating seeds; Cactus moth introduced in Australia
to control invasive cactus.
● Cactus moth introduced in Australia to control invasive cactus is an example of a biological control method that involves using a predator or parasite to control pest populations. It **must be remembered that biological control methods** **adopted in agricultural pest control are based on the ability of the predator** **to control prey population**. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
●  **Mutualism (+, +):** o Mutualism is an interaction where **both species benefit** from the association, often involving
close physical and functional relationships. o It promotes **coevolution**, supports **pollination, seed dispersal**, and **nutrient exchange**,
contributing to species survival and ecosystem resilience. o **Examples**: Lichens (algae + fungi), Mycorrhiza (fungus + plant roots), Fig tree and fig wasp
(species-specific pollination), Ophrys orchid mimicking female bee to attract male for pollination (sexual mimicry).
● Mycorrhizae is a mutualistic association where the fungus helps the plant absorb nutrients and water, and the plant supplies carbohydrates to the fungus. **So, Pair III** **is correctly matched.**
●  **Commensalism (+, 0):** o Commensalism is an interaction where **one species benefits**, while the **other remains**
**unaffected**—neither harmed nor helped. o It enhances access to resources or space for the benefitting species without impacting the
host species. o **Examples**: **Orchids growing on mango trees to access sunlight**, Cattle egrets feeding
on insects disturbed by grazing cattle, Barnacles attached to whales for mobility and feeding.
● Orchids growing on mango trees benefit by gaining support and access to sunlight, while the mango tree is neither harmed nor benefited **So, pair II is correctly** **matched.**
● **Ammensalism (–, 0):** o **Ammensalism** is an interaction **where one species is harmed or inhibited, while the**
**other species remains unaffected**. This usually occurs when one organism produces a substance that negatively impacts another without any benefit or harm to itself. o This interaction **often influences population dynamics and community structure** by
suppressing certain species, thereby indirectly shaping ecosystem diversity.
o **Examples:** Penicillium mold secreting penicillin, large trees shading smaller plants beneath
them, limiting their growth without gaining any benefit.
● Fungus producing antibiotics inhibits or kills bacteria or other microbes. **So, Pair IV** **is correctly matched** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
●  **Competition (–, –):** o Competition is an interaction where **two or more species compete** for
the same limited resource such as food, space, light, or mates, which negatively affects both. o This shapes community structure and can lead to **competitive exclusion**
(Gause's principle) or **resource partitioning**, promoting species coexistence. o **Examples**: Flamingos and fish competing for zooplankton in a lake,
Barnacles competing for space on rocky shores and five species of warblers foraging in different parts of a tree (resource partitioning)
●  **Parasitism (+, –):** o Parasitism is an interaction in which one species, the **parasite**, lives in or on
another species, the **host**, deriving nourishment and shelter while harming the host. o It influences **host population health**, drives **coevolution**, and often
involves **complex life cycles** and **host specificity**. o **Examples**: Head lice on humans (ectoparasite), Tapeworm inside human
intestine (endoparasite), Koel laying eggs in crow’s nest (brood parasitism)

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Major Species (Keystone, Indicator, Invasive, Endemic, etc.)
questions.questionText:
The term 'Evil Quartet', sometimes seen in the
news in the context of environment, describes a set of causes for which of the following?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Scientist Jarod Diamond in 1984, whilst studying the cause of extinction of species over the course of planetary history, identified **four key factors which are central to every major extinction that** **has happened over the geological time.**
● He termed these four phenomena as ‘four horsemen of the ecological apocalypse’ or the **evil quartet**,
which though unrelated but are contributing to the extinction of species across the planet, thus leading to the loss of biodiversity.
● The four factors included in the Evil Quartet are:
● **Habitat loss and fragmentation:** It is the most significant cause of biodiversity loss. Rampant destruction of critical habitats leads to the loss and extinction of endemic species dependent on habitat, also shrinkage of the habitat area leads to increased interspecific and intraspecific competition contributing to extinction of species.
● **Over-exploitation:** Excessive exploitation of biological resources by humans, more than what it could replace naturally, is referred to as overexploitation. Many species extinctions in the last 500 years for ex. Steller's sea cow. Passenger pigeons were due to overexploitation by humans. Presently many marine fish populations around the world are being over harvested, endangering the continued existence of commercially important species.
● **Alien species invasion:** Introduction of non-native species into a habitat leads to it competing for resources with the native species. As these alien species don't have a natural predator in their host
environment, they often take over the major resources of the habitat, pushing the native species towards extinction. For example, Latana camara, a native of South America which was introduced in India for its exotic and vibrant flowers, has been a major cause for the destruction of Nilgiri forests.
● **Co-extinctions:** When an species becomes extinct, all the plants and animal species associated with it in an obligatory way also become extinct, for example coevolved plant and pollinator, which exhibit mutualism extinction of one invariably leads to the extinction of the other. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. Pyramid of Number is always upright.
II. Pyramid of Biomass is always upright.
III. Pyramid of Energy is never inverted.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Ecological pyramids are **graphical representations** to show the **relationship between producers** **and consumers** at different trophic levels in terms of number, biomass and energy.
● **Generally, the base of a pyramid is broad and it narrows towards the apex** when   expressing the food or energy relationship between organisms at different trophic levels. The **base of each** **pyramid represents the producers** or the first trophic level while the **apex represents tertiary** **or top-level consumer.**
● Broadly there are three types of Ecological Pyramids: o **Pyramid of Number:** It is a graphical representation of the **number of individuals** at each
trophic level. In most of the ecosystems it **stays upright**, with the primary producers occupying the broad base, **but in case of tree ecosystem it is inverted.** **So, statement I is not correct.**
▪ In the tree ecosystem, a single tree (primary producer) is supporting a bunch of organisms
(primary consumers) nesting on it, these organisms and nests are then inhabited by even more numbers of microorganisms, as pests. **Hence, the pyramid of number in case of tree** **ecosystem becomes inverted**. o **Pyramid of biomass:**
▪ It depicts **total dry weight** (or organic matter) of all organisms at each trophic level. It is
generally **upright in terrestrial ecosystems** (e.g., forest or grassland). But gets **inverted** **in aquatic ecosystems**, where the **standing biomass of phytoplankton** is less than **that** **of zooplankton/fish** because phytoplankton reproduce and are consumed rapidly. **So,** **statement II is not correct.** o **Pyramid of Energy:**
▪ **Always upright** due to unidirectional flow of energy and progressive energy loss at each
trophic level (mostly as heat). Cannot be inverted because energy input from one level to the next is always less than what is consumed**. So, statement III is correct.** **So, only one of the above given statements is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Which of the following conditions favour the
process of decomposition?
I. 	 Anaerobic conditions
II. 	 Low temperatures
III. 	Moist and humid environment
IV. 	 Nitrogen rich base material
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Breaking down the complex organic matter** into inorganic substances like **carbon dioxide,** **water and nutrients** and the process is called **decomposition.**
● Complex organic matters include **dead plant remains such as leaves, bark, flowers and dead** **remains of animals, including faecal matter.**
● They **constitute detritus, which is the raw material for decomposition**. The processes involved are fragmentation, leaching, catabolism, humification and mineralisation.
o **Fragmentation:** Detritivores (e.g., earthworm) break down detritus into smaller particles.
o **Leaching:** Water soluble inorganic nutrients go down into the soil horizon and get precipitated
as unavailable salts. o **Catabolism:** Bacterial and fungal enzymes degrade detritus into simpler inorganic substances. o **Humification:** It leads to accumulation of a dark coloured amorphous substance called humus.
It is highly resistant to microbial action and undergoes decomposition at an extremely slow rate. Being colloidal in nature it serves as a reservoir of nutrients. o **Mineralisation:** The humus is further degraded by some microbes and release of inorganic
nutrients occur. **Factors Affecting Decomposition:**
● **Presence of Oxygen:** o Decomposition is **primarily an aerobic activity** and is influenced by both the chemical
nature of detritus and climatic conditions. Anaerobic conditions (**lack of oxygen)** slow microbial activity.
▪ **Anaerobic microbes work slower** and produce gases like methane and hydrogen
sulfide. This **leads to incomplete decomposition** and accumulation of organic matter (e.g., peat in bogs). **So, point I is not correct.**
● **Temperature:** o Among climatic factors, **temperature and soil moisture play a crucial role** by affecting
microbial activity in the soil. **Low temperature reduces microbial and enzymatic** **activity.** It slows or halts decomposition and the organic matter remains preserved (e.g., permafrost regions). **So, point II is not correct.**
● **Moist and humid conditions:** o **It enhances decomposition by** supporting microbial growth and enzyme function enabling
quicker breakdown of nutrients and recycling. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Nitrogen Content:** o **Nitrogen** is essential for **microbial protein synthesis and metabolism**, making
nitrogen-rich materials like **green leaves decompose much faster.** **So, point IV is** **correct.** o In contrast, **detritus with high lignin or chitin content**, such as wood or bark, is more
resistant to microbial attack and breaks down slowly. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Ex-Situ and In-Situ Conservation
questions.questionText:
Consider the following methods of biodiversity
conservation:
I. 	 Sacred Grooves
II. 	 Zoological Parks
III. 	Herbarium
IV. 	 National Parks
V. 	 Seed Banks
How many of the above are methods of in situ
conservation?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Conservation is the protection, preservation, management, or restoration of wildlife and natural resources such as forests and water. Through the conservation of biodiversity and the survival of many species and habitats which are threatened due to human activities can be ensured.
● Conservation can broadly be divided into **two types**: In-situ conservation and Ex-situ conservation. o **In-situ** **conservation:** It is **on site** conservation or the conservation of genetic resources in
natural populations of plant or animal species, such as forest genetic resources in natural populations of tree species. It is the **process of protecting an endangered plant or animal** **species in its natural habitat**, either by protecting or cleaning up the habitat itself, or by defending the species from predators. o **Ex-situ** **conservation:** It is the preservation of the components of biological diversity **outside**
**their natural habitats**. This involves conservation of genetic resources, as well as wild and cultivated species, and draws on a diverse body of techniques and facilities. Such strategies include
establishment of botanical gardens, zoos, conservation strands and gene, pollen seed, seedling, tissue culture and DNA banks.
● **Sacred Grooves** are forest areas preserved due to religious or cultural traditions. They are excellent examples of **in-situ** **conservation**, as the species are protected in their natural settings. **So, point I is** **correct.**
● **Zoological parks**: It houses animals in artificial environments away from their natural habitats. This is a form of **ex-situ** **conservation**. **So, point II is not correct.**
● **Herbarium:** It consists of preserved plant specimens, stored and studied away from their natural habitats. This is **ex-situ** **conservation**. **So, point III is not correct.**
● **National Parks**: These are designated areas where wildlife is conserved in its natural environment. National parks are classic examples of **in-situ** **conservation. So, point IV is correct.**
● **Seed banks**: These are storage facilities where seeds are preserved under controlled conditions. This method falls under **ex-situ** **conservation. So, point V is not correct.** **So, only two of the above given points are methods of** **in situ** **conservation.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Which one of the following organelles is **not**
found in animal cells?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A typical eukaryotic cell consists of a cell membrane, nucleus and cytoplasm. Plant cells have a cell wall outside the cell membrane.
● Cells of **bacteria, algae, fungi and higher plants** are also surrounded by a **rigid cell wall** besides the plasma membrane. **It is not found in animal cells.** It differs structurally in bacteria and eukaryotes. In bacteria, it is composed of **polysaccharide** cross-linked by small peptides, which provides rigidity, shape and protection from osmotic pressure; and in eukaryotes (plants and fungi), it is primarily made up of **polysaccharides**. Cell wall not only determines the cell shape, but also **prevents cell bursting** **caused due to osmotic pressure.** It also helps in cell–cell interaction and provides mechanical strength and protection from infection. **Gram-positive bacteria** have a thick cell wall with the single plasma membrane
**Animal Cell**                                                          **Plant cells** **Therefore, option (c) is the answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Cell membrane (Plasma membrane):** Each cell has a limiting boundary, the cell membrane, plasma membrane or plasmalemma. It is a living membrane, outermost in animal cells but internal to the cell wall in plant cells. It is flexible and can fold in (as in food vacuoles of Amoeba) or fold out (as in the formation of pseudopodia of Amoeba).
● **Vacuoles** are membrane-bound intracellular organelle found in the cytoplasm of most plants, fungi and also some **animal cells.** The number and size of **vacuoles varies per plant cell.** Young cells have a large number of small vacuoles. As the plant cell matures, the vacuoles amalgamate to form a large central vacuole that occupies almost 90 per cent of the cytoplasmic volume.
● **Lysosomes** are present in almost **all animal cells and some non-green plant cells.** They perform intracellular digestion. Lysosomes are called **“suicidal bags”** as enzymes contained in them can digest the cell’s own material when damaged or dead.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the viruses:
I. 	 Viruses are capable of directly invading the
Central Nervous System (CNS) tissue.
II. 	 Bacterial viruses are usually double-stranded
DNA viruses.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **virus** is an infectious agent of small size and simple composition that can **multiply only in living** **cells of animals, plants, or bacteria.** It consists of genetic material, either DNA or RNA, enclosed within a protective protein shell called a capsid.
o In addition to proteins, viruses also contain genetic material, that could be either RNA or DNA**.**
A virus is a nucleoprotein and the genetic material is infectious. o **Bacterial viruses or bacteriophages** (viruses that infect the bacteria) are **usually double-**
**stranded DNA viruses** because double-stranded DNA offers genetic stability, efficient integration into host genomes and direct compatibility with bacterial replication and transcription systems. **So, statement II is correct.** o In general, viruses that infect plants have single stranded RNA and viruses that infect **animals**
**have** **either single or double stranded RNA** or double stranded DNA. o **Viruses are capable of directly invading the Central Nervous System (CNS) tissue,**
leading to a range of neurological diseases. They can reach the CNS through several mechanisms, such as traveling along **peripheral nerves, crossing the blood-brain barrier,** or infecting immune cells that migrate into the brain. Viruses like herpes simplex virus, rabies virus, HIV, West Nile virus and enteroviruses can infect and damage neurons and other CNS cells,
sometimes causing severe inflammation, encephalitis, or meningitis. **So, statement I is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Which one of the following best describes a
Virtual Private Network (VPN)?

options.a:
A service that allows multiple users to access
the same IP address for efficient bandwidth usage
options.b:
A secure, encrypted connection established
over a public network to protect data transmission
options.c: A cloud-based platform for virtual collaboration among distributed teams
options.d:
A network protocol used to convert private IP
addresses into public ones for internet access

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **Virtual Private Network (VPN)** is a technology that establishes a secure and private connection over a public network between a user's device and the internet. This **encrypted** "tunnel" protects data from being intercepted, **masks the user's IP addres**s, and allows for **private and anonymous** browsing.
● **How a VPN Works:** ○ When we use a VPN, our internet traffic is routed through a remote server operated by the VPN provider. ○ The data is encrypted before it leaves your device, travels securely through the internet and is decrypted only at the VPN server or the intended destination. ○ This process **hides your real IP address** and online activities from your Internet Service Provider (ISP), hackers and other third parties.
● **Benefits of VPN:** ○ **Privacy:** VPNs protect browsing history and personal data from being tracked or monitored. ○ **Security:** Encryption safeguards information, especially on public Wi-Fi networks. ○ **Anonymity:** VPNs help maintain online anonymity. ○ **Access:** VPNs can bypass geographic restrictions and censorship by making it appear as if you are browsing from another location. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Gonorrhea
II. 	 Pyelonephritis
III. 	Tuberculosis
IV. 	 Hepatitis B
V. 	 Human Immunodeficiency Virus
How many of the above are Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Infections or diseases which are transmitted through sexual intercourse are collectively called **Sexually** **Transmitted Infections (STIs)** or Venereal Diseases (VD) or Reproductive Tract Infections (RTIs).
○ **Gonorrhea** is a common STI caused by bacteria, often presenting with genital discharge and painful urination. It also increases the risk of HIV infection. **So, point I is correct.** ○ **Pyelonephritis** is not a sexually transmitted infection; it is a type of urinary tract infection that affects the kidneys. **So point II is not correct.** ○ **Tuberculosis** is a serious illness that mainly affects the lungs. The germs that cause tuberculosis are a **type of bacteria**. Tuberculosis can spread when a person with the illness coughs, sneezes or sings. **So, point III is not correct.** ○ **Hepatitis B** is a serious liver infection caused by the **Hepatitis B Virus (HBV).** It is most commonly transmitted from mother to child during birth and delivery, in early childhood, as well as through contact with **blood** or other **body fluids during sex** with an infected partner, unsafe injections or exposures to sharp instruments. **So, point IV is correct.** ○ **Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV)** is a sexually transmitted infection that weakens the immune system. Without treatment, it can develop into Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome (AIDS). **So, point V is correct.**
● Some other examples of STIs are genital herpes, genital warts, trichomoniasis, Syphilis  and chlamydiasis. **So, only three of the above are Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs).** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Name of Vitamin
Source Deficiency Disease
I. - Vitamin A Dairy products
Night blindness
II. - Vitamin B2 Green vegetables
Skin diseases
III. Vitamin K Cereals Anaemia
IV. Vitamin D Oily fish Rickets In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The term **“vitamin”** is used to describe certain **organic compounds** that are needed by the body but that **cannot be manufactured by the body.** They mainly serve as catalysts for certain reactions in the body. The amounts of vitamins required are very small, perhaps hundreds of grams.
● Vitamins are divided into two groups–fat-soluble vitamins (Vitamins A, D, E, and K) and water-soluble vitamins (Vitamins of the B group and Vitamin C).
● **Vitamin A**, also called **retinol,** has many functions in the body. In addition to helping the eyes adjust to light changes, vitamin A plays an important role in bone growth, tooth development, reproduction, cell division, gene expression and regulation of the immune system.
○ The retinol, retinal, and retinoic acid forms of vitamin A are supplied primarily by **foods of** **animal origin such as dairy products, fish and liver.** Some foods of plant origin contain the antioxidant beta-carotene, which the body converts to vitamin A. ○ **Vitamin A deficiency is rare,** but the disease that results is known as **xerophthalmia**. It most commonly occurs in developing nations, usually due to malnutrition. Since vitamin A is stored in the liver, it may take up to 2 years for signs of deficiency to appear. **Night blindness** **and very dry,** rough skin may indicate a lack of vitamin A. **So, row I is correctly matched.**
● **Riboflavin, or vitamin B2,** helps to release energy from foods, promotes good vision, and healthy skin. It also helps to convert the amino acid tryptophan (which makes up protein) into niacin.
○ Sources include **liver, eggs, dark green vegetables, legumes, whole and enriched grain** **products and milk.** Ultraviolet light is known to destroy riboflavin, which is why most milk is packaged in opaque containers instead of clear. ○ **Deficiency Diseases:** **Skin diseases** and retarded growth. Symptoms of deficiency include cracks at the corners of the mouth, dermatitis on the nose and lips, light sensitivity, cataracts and a sore, red tongue. **So, row II is correctly matched.**
● **Vitamin K** is naturally produced by the **bacteria in the intestines** and plays an essential role in normal blood clotting, promoting bone health and helping to produce proteins for blood, bones, and kidneys.
○ Good food sources of vitamin K are green, leafy vegetables such as **turnip greens, spinach,** **cauliflower, cabbage and broccoli** and certain vegetable oils including soybean oil, cottonseed oil, canola oil and olive oil. Animal foods, in general, contain limited amounts of vitamin K. ○ **Deficiency Diseases:** **Excessive bleeding from wounds. So, row III is not correctly** **matched.**
● **Vitamin D** plays a critical role in the body’s use of **calcium and phosphorus**. It works by increasing the amount of calcium absorbed from the small intestine, helping to form and maintain bones. Vitamin D benefits the body by playing a role in immunity and controlling cell growth.
○ The primary food sources of vitamin D are **milk and other dairy products** fortified with vitamin D. Vitamin D is also found in **oily fish** (e.g., herring, salmon and sardines) as well as in cod liver oil. In addition to the vitamin D provided by food, we obtain vitamin D through our skin, which produces vitamin D in response to sunlight. ○ Symptoms of vitamin D deficiency in growing children include **rickets** (long, soft, bowed legs) and flattening of the back of the skull. Vitamin D deficiency in adults may result in osteomalacia (muscle and bone weakness) and osteoporosis (loss of bone mass). **So, row IV is correctly** **matched.** **So, in only three of the above given rows is the information correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Fat-Soluble Vitamins:** ○ **Vitamin A:** ■ **Sources:** Milk, butter, egg, carrots, cod liver oil, tomatoes, pumpkin, green
leafy vegetables ■ **Need:** Normal growth and to keep eyes and skin healthy ■ **Deficiency Diseases:** Night blindness, Irregular growth of teeth, **so, row**
**I is correctly matched.** ○ **Vitamin D:** ■ **Sources:** Cheese, butter, milk, green vegetables, fish liver oil, sunlight ■ **Need:** Formation of strong bones and teeth ■ **Deficiency Diseases:** Rickets. ○ **Vitamin E:** ■ **Sources:** Vegetable oils, spinach, lettuce, turnip leaves, butter, milk, whole
grains, nuts, seeds, leafy vegetables ■ **Need:** Protection of the cell membrane, formation of red blood cells (RBC) ■ **Deficiency Diseases:** Not yet confirmed ○ **Vitamin K:** ■ **Sources:** Cabbage, cauliflower, spinach and other green leafy vegetables,
cereals, soybeans ■ **Need:** Clotting of blood ■ **Deficiency Diseases:** Excessive bleeding from wounds.
● **Water-Soluble Vitamins:** ○ **Vitamin B1 (Thiamin):** ■ **Sources:** Seafood, milk, meat, peas, cereals, green vegetables ■ **Need:** Growth and development, releasing energy from carbohydrates ■ **Deficiency Diseases:** Beri beri ○ **Vitamin B3 (Niacin):** ■ **Sources:** Whole cereals, tomatoes, potatoes, meat and fish ■ **Need:** Healthy skin, digestion and nervous system ■ **Deficiency Diseases:** Pellagra (affects skin, alimentary canal and nervous
system) ○ **Vitamin B12:** ■ **Sources:** Liver, milk, eggs, fish ■ **Need:** Forming RBC ■ **Deficiency Diseases:** Anaemia ○ **Vitamin C:** ■ **Sources:** Indian gooseberry (amla), lemons, lime, pineapple, oranges,
watermelon, green peppers, tomatoes, cabbage, turnip, carrots, green mint, guava ■ **Need:** Maintenance of ligaments, tendons, other supportive tissues and
blood vessels ■ **Deficiency Diseases:** Scurvy (gums swell and bleed).

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 All metals except mercury exist as solids at
room temperature.
II. 	 Diamond has a very high melting and boiling
point.
III. 	Alkali metals have high densities and high
melting points.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Metal** is a solid material which is **hard, shiny, malleable, fusible and ductile**, with good electrical and thermal conductivity. They are **electropositive in nature.** They generally have 1, 2 or 3 electrons in their valence shells and readily lose these electrons to form positively charged ions (cations). These cations are stable as they acquire a noble gas configuration after losing the valence shell electrons.
● **All metals except mercury exist as solids at room temperature. So, statement I is correct.**
● Carbon is a non-metal that can exist in different forms. Each form is called an allotrope. **Diamond,** an allotrope of carbon, is the hardest natural substance known and **has a very high melting and boiling** **point.** Graphite, another allotrope of carbon, is a conductor of electricity. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Alkaline metals** (lithium, sodium, potassium) are so soft that they can be cut with a knife. They have **low densities and low melting points.** **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● Mercury is the only metal and bromine is the only non-metal which exists in a liquid state at room temperature.
● Graphite and iodine possess metallic lustre, though they are non-metals.
● Sodium metal is soft like wax and can be cut with a knife.
● Gallium metal would melt if kept on our palm.
● Gold and silver are the most malleable and ductile metals.
● Diamond is a better conductor of heat than copper but a poor conductor of electricity.
● Graphite is the only non-metal which is a good conductor of electricity but a poor conductor of heat.
● Gold, silver, platinum and copper are found in the free state due to their poor reactivity compared to other metals.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Oil rises through a cotton wick
II. 	 Water wets human skin but does not wet duck
feathers
III. 	Mercury does not adhere to glass, whereas
water clings to it
IV. 	 Oil and water do not mix
How many of the above phenomena can be primarily explained by the concept of surface tension?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Surface tension** is a fundamental physical phenomenon that arises due to **intermolecular forces at** **the free surface of a liquid**. It manifests as a contractive tendency of a **liquid surface to minimize** **its area,** owing to the excess energy possessed by surface molecules compared to those in the bulk. As a result, liquids behave as if their surfaces are under tension.
● When **oil touches a cotton wick**, it climbs up through the tiny spaces between the threads, despite gravity trying to pull it down. This happens because the oil sticks more strongly to the cotton than to itself. The **surface tension of the oil helps pull other oil molecules upward**. This pulling effect, known as **capillary** **action**, occurs because of surface tension—the liquid tries to spread and move in a way that requires the least amount of energy.  **So, point I is correct.**
● **Duck feathers** are covered with a **waterproof oily layer** that stops water from sticking. This is because surface tension causes water to hold itself together more than it wants to stick to the feathers. But on our skin, the opposite happens—water sticks more than it pulls on itself. **So, point II is correct.**
● If you pour mercury into a glass container, it **forms round blobs and doesn’t stick to the glass.** But water spreads and clings to the glass walls. That’s because mercury molecules pull on each other more strongly than they pull on the glass, so they stay together. Water, on the other hand, pulls more on the glass than on itself, so it spreads out. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Oil and water do not mix** because their molecules are different and don t attract each other well. This creates a boundary between them, where surface tension keeps them separated. Oil forms droplets or floats on water because it tries to minimize contact with water. **So, point IV is correct.** **So, all the four given phenomena can be primarily explained by the concept of surface tension.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Shaving mirrors
II. 	 Rear-view mirrors in vehicles
III. 	Vehicles headlights
IV. 	 Torches
How many of the above are applications of convex mirrors?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The reflecting surface of a spherical mirror may be curved inwards or outwards. A spherical mirror, whose reflecting surface is **curved inwards,** that is, faces towards the centre of the sphere, is called a **concave mirror.** A **spherical mirror** whose reflecting surface is **curved outwards,** is called a **convex mirror.**
● **Convex mirrors** are commonly used as **rear-view** **(wing) mirrors in vehicles.** These mirrors are fitted on the sides of the vehicle, enabling the driver to see traffic behind him/her to facilitate safe driving. Convex mirrors are preferred because they always give an erect, though diminished, image. Also, they have a wider field of view as they are curved outwards. Thus, convex mirrors enable the driver to view much larger area than would be possible with a plane mirror. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Concave mirrors** are commonly used in **torches, search-lights and vehicle headlights** to get powerful parallel beams of light. They are often used as **shaving mirrors** to see a larger image of the face. The dentists use concave mirrors to see large images of the teeth of patients. Large concave mirrors are used to concentrate sunlight to produce heat in solar furnaces. **So, points I, III and IV are not** **correct.** **So, only one of the above is the application of convex mirrors.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
A chemistry student sets up two identical conical
flasks with equal volumes of dilute hydrochloric acid (HCl) at the same temperature. In Flask A, she adds a small strip of zinc metal, while in Flask B, she adds a larger strip of zinc of the same thickness. She observes that the reaction in Flask B is faster, releasing hydrogen gas more rapidly.
Which of the following best explains why the rate of reaction is higher in Flask B?

options.a:
The molecular weight of zinc in Flask B is
higher
options.b: Zinc in Flask B is more reactive than in Flask A
options.c:
A higher surface area of zinc increases the
rate at the same temperature
options.d:
The color and odor of the acid influenced the
reaction

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● When a metal like **zinc reacts with hydrochloric acid**, the reaction occurs at the  surface where the metal comes into contact with the acid:
● In Flask B, the **larger strip of zinc offers more surface area** for the acid to collide with the zinc atoms.
● More surface area means more frequent effective collisions, which leads to a **higher rate of hydrogen** **gas evolution.**
● **The rate of reaction** refers to how **fast reactants convert to products.** It can be described as: ○ Initially, only reactants are present. As the reaction proceeds, their concentration decreases and the product concentration increases. ○ The rate of a reaction typically decreases over time as reactants get used up. ○ However, at the start, if the **concentration or surface area of reactants is higher, the** **reaction proceeds faster.**
○ The **rate of reaction increases in Flask B** because the **larger zinc strip has more surface** **area,** allowing more acid particles to collide with the metal atoms per unit time, increasing the frequency of effective collisions, which **directly affects the rate.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following diseases:
I. 	 Kala-Azar
II. 	 Smallpox
III. 	Leprosy
IV. 	 Lymphatic Filariasis
How many of the above has/have been eradicated in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Eradication** refers to the **permanent global reduction to zero** of the incidence of a disease due to deliberate efforts, after which **no further interventions are required**.
● **Elimination** means reducing the disease **to very low levels within a specific area or country**, though **continued interventions are still needed** to prevent its resurgence.
● **Kala-azar**, also known as **visceral leishmaniasis**, is caused by Leishmania parasites transmitted through sandflies. In India, kala-azar has **not been eradicated**, but it is part of the **elimination** **programme** under the **National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme (NVBDCP)**. Elimination of kala-azar is defined as having **less than 1 case per 10,000 population at the block** **level**. India has made significant progress and most endemic districts have achieved this target, but efforts are still ongoing. **So, point I is not correct.**
● **Smallpox** is the **only human disease that has been globally eradicated**. It is caused by the **variola** **virus** and was eliminated through an intensive global vaccination campaign led by the **World Health** **Organisation (WHO)**. In India, the last case was reported in **1975** and the country was declared **smallpox-free in 1977**. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Leprosy**, caused by Mycobacterium leprae, is a chronic infectious disease affecting skin and nerves. India achieved the **elimination of leprosy as a public health problem at the national level in 2005**,
which means **less than 1 case per 10,000 population**. New cases are still being detected annually and the disease continues to exist in some pockets. **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Lymphatic Filariasis (LF)**, commonly known as **filaria**, is caused by parasitic worms transmitted by mosquitoes. India is implementing the **Elimination of Lymphatic Filariasis Programme**, aiming to reduce the **microfilaria rate to less than 1%** in endemic areas. Although significant progress has been made, complete interruption of transmission has not yet been achieved in all areas. **So, point IV is not** **correct.** **So, only one of the above has been eradicated in India.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
